  • Deficiency causes: FAT MAL = Fat malabsorption (CF, cholestasis), Abetalipoproteinaemia (MTTP), TTPA mutation (AVED), M (MCT? no — malabsorption syndromes)
  • Neuro triad: ANR = Ataxia (spinocerebellar), Neuropathy (peripheral), Retinopathy (pigmented)
  • Abetalipo vs AVED: ABETALIPO = NO APOB, LOW CHOL; AVED = NORMAL LIPOPROTEINS, TTPA
  • SELECT trial: VIT E + SE → ↑PROSTATE CANCER (35k men, stopped early)
  • Mortality meta-analysis: >400 IU/day → ↑ALL-CAUSE MORTALITY (Miller 2005, Cochrane 2018)
  • Warfarin interaction: VIT E + WARFARIN = ↑INR (monitor)
  • Statins + Niacin + Antioxidants: VIT E/C/β-CAROTENE BLUNT HDL₂ RISE (ARBITER, HATS)

8. 8. Mind Map

Vitamin E
Isoforms
Tocopherols (saturated tail)
– α, β, γ, δ (methylation)
Tocotrienols (unsaturated tail)
– α, β, γ, δ (better membrane mobility)
Bioavailability
RRR-α-toc (natural) 2x all-rac (synthetic)
α-TTP selects α-toc → VLDL secretion
γ/δ metabolised by CYP4F2 → excreted
Antioxidant Mechanism
Lipid peroxidation chain
– LOO• + α-TOH → LOOH + α-TO•
Recycling
– α-TO• + Vit C → α-TOH
– GSH → Vit C
Membrane protection
– 1 α-toc / 200–500 phospholipids
– Protects PUFA in LDL, membranes
Non-antioxidant
– Gene expression, PKC inhibition, Platelet inhibition
Deficiency
Causes
– Fat malabsorption (CF, cholestasis, short bowel)
– Abetalipoproteinaemia (MTTP → no apoB)
– AVED (TTPA → ↓α-TTP)
– Preterm infants
Clinical Triad
– Spinocerebellar ataxia
– Peripheral neuropathy (axonal, sensory)
– Pigmented retinopathy
Specific Syndromes
– Abetalipoproteinaemia: MTTP, absent apoB, acanthocytes, retinitis, coagulopathy
– AVED: TTPA, normal lipids, plasma vit E <5%, Tx: 800–1200 IU/day RRR
– Preterm: Haemolytic anaemia → Tx 10–20 IU/kg/day
Toxicity / High-Dose Risks
UL: 1000 mg (1500 IU natural)
Chronic >400 IU/day
– ↑All-cause mortality (Miller 2005, Cochrane 2018)
– ↑Haemorrhagic stroke (HPFS/WHS)
– ↑Prostate cancer (SELECT HR 1.17)
– ↑Heart failure
Mechanism
– ↓γ-tocopherol (α-TTP competition)
– Pro-oxidant (tocopheroxyl radical)
– Antiplatelet → bleeding
Drug Interactions
– Warfarin: ↑INR
– Statins + Niacin: ↓HDL₂ rise
– Antiplatelets: ↑bleeding
– Chemo: ↓efficacy (theoretical)
Special Populations
Preterm: 10–20 IU/kg/day
CF/Cholestasis: 15–25 IU/kg/day water-miscible
Abetalipo: 100–200 IU/kg/day
AVED: 800–1200 IU/day RRR lifelong
Pregnancy: RDA 15 mg, avoid high-dose

9. 9. -Hour Recall Prompts

  1. List 8 vitamin E isoforms; which is preferentially retained by α-TTP?
  2. Draw antioxidant cycle: LOO• + α-TOH → LOOH + α-TO• → recycled by vit C/GSH
  3. Neurological triad of vit E deficiency: Ataxia, Neuropathy, Retinopathy
  4. Difference abetalipoproteinaemia (MTTP, no apoB) vs AVED (TTPA, normal lipids)
  5. AVED treatment: high-dose RRR-α-toc 800–1200 IU/day lifelong
  6. SELECT trial: vit E 400 IU → ↑prostate cancer HR 1.17
  7. Meta-analysis >400 IU/day → ↑all-cause mortality (Miller 2005, Cochrane 2018)
  8. Warfarin interaction: vit E ↑INR (antiplatelet + ?carboxylase inhibition)

11. 11. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴8 isoforms, α-TTP mechanism, antioxidant chain-breaking mechanism, deficiency only in fat malabsorption, neurological triad (ataxia/neuropathy/retinopathy), AVED (TTPA, normal lipids) treatment, high-dose risks (mortality, prostate cancer, stroke), warfarin interaction
Should Know 🟡γ-tocopherol vs α-tocopherol roles, tocotrienol potency, preterm haemolytic anaemia, abetalipoproteinaemia details, statin+niacin interaction, cisplatin/cyclosporine interactions
Nice to Know 🟢CYP4F2 metabolism, specific tocotrienol studies, vit E in neurodegeneration (Alzheimer's), topical vit E for scars (myth), genetic variants (CYP4F2, SCARB1, TTPA)

12. 12. My Weak Points

  • [ ] Tocotrienol-specific clinical trials
  • [ ] γ-tocopherol RNS trapping mechanism details
  • [ ] Exact warfarin-vit E interaction mechanism (carboxylase vs antiplatelet)

13. 13. Self-Test Scorecard

DomainScore /10Target /10
Understanding8+
Recall8+
MCQ Performance8+
SBA Performance8+
Viva Confidence8+
TOTAL40+/50

14. 14. Exam Answer Modes

1. Long Answer / Essay (20 min)

Topic: "Vitamin E: isoforms, antioxidant function, deficiency syndromes, and risks of supplementation"

  • 8 isoforms: 4 tocopherols (saturated) + 4 tocotrienols (unsaturated); α-tocopherol preferential retention by α-TTP
  • Antioxidant mechanism: chain-breaking lipid peroxidation (LOO• + α-TOH → LOOH + α-TO•); recycling by vitamin C/GSH
  • Deficiency ONLY in fat malabsorption: CF, cholestasis, abetalipoproteinaemia (MTTP → no apoB), AVED (TTPA → ↓α-TTP), preterm
  • Neurological triad: spinocerebellar ataxia, peripheral neuropathy, pigmented retinopathy
  • Abetalipoproteinaemia vs AVED: lipids absent vs normal; genes MTTP vs TTPA
  • High-dose risks: meta-analyses ↑mortality >400 IU/day (Miller 2005, Cochrane 2018); SELECT ↑prostate cancer; ↑haemorrhagic stroke; mechanism: pro-oxidant, displaces γ-tocopherol, antiplatelet
  • Drug interactions: warfarin (↑INR), statins+niacin (blunts HDL₂), antiplatelets (bleeding), chemo (theoretical)

2. Short Note (7 min)

Topic: "Abetalipoproteinaemia vs Isolated Vitamin E Deficiency (AVED)"

  • Abetalipoproteinaemia: MTTP mutation (AR) → no MTP → no chylomicron/VLDL assembly → absent apoB, LDL, VLDL; cholesterol <50 mg/dL; fat malabsorption + vit A/D/E/K deficiency; acanthocytosis, retinitis pigmentosa, ataxia, coagulopathy. Treatment: high-dose fat-soluble vitamins (vit E 100–200 IU/kg/day), MCT oil, fat restriction.
  • AVED: TTPA mutation (AR, chr 8) → ↓α-TTP → α-tocopherol not secreted in VLDL; normal lipids/lipoproteins; plasma α-toc <5% normal; onset 5–15y ataxia, neuropathy, retinopathy. Treatment: RRR-α-tocopherol 800–1200 IU/day lifelong → halts progression.

3. Viva Answer (3 min)

Q: "Why is vitamin E supplementation not recommended for cardiovascular prevention?"

"A: No benefit, possible harm. Large RCTs (HOPE-TOO, GISSI-P, PHS II) and meta-analyses (Cochrane 2018, 78 RCTs, 299k pts) show no reduction in CVD events; Cochrane: vit E ↑all-cause mortality RR 1.03. Proposed mechanisms: high-dose α-tocopherol displaces γ-tocopherol (loss of RNS trapping), pro-oxidant tocopheroxyl radical, antiplatelet effect. Do not prescribe for CVD prevention."

4. Ward Case Discussion (5 min)

Case: 8-year-old boy, progressive gait ataxia, areflexia, Babinski+, night blindness, normal lipid profile, plasma α-tocopherol 0.8 µmol/L (ref 11–37).

"Diagnosis: AVED (TTPA mutation). Confirm with genetic testing. Treatment: RRR-α-tocopherol 1200 IU/day (800–1500 IU) oral lifelong. Expect neurological stabilisation/improvement in 3–6 months. Refer for genetic counselling (AR, siblings 25% risk). Monitor plasma α-tocopherol target >20 µmol/L."

5. Last-Night-Before-Exam Sheet (1 min)

  • Isoforms: 4 tocopherols + 4 tocotrienols (α/β/γ/δ each); α-tocopherol biologically active
  • α-TTP: Liver protein → binds α-tocopherol → incorporates into VLDL; TTPA mutation = AVED
  • Antioxidant: LOO• + α-TOH → LOOH + α-TO• → recycled by vit C/GSH
  • Deficiency: ONLY fat malabsorption (CF, cholestasis, abetalipo, AVED, preterm)
  • Triad: Ataxia (spinocerebellar), Neuropathy (axonal sensory), Retinopathy (pigmented)
  • Abetalipo (MTTP): No apoB, low cholesterol, acanthocytes, all fat-soluble vit deficiency
  • AVED (TTPA): Normal lipids, plasma vit E <5%, Tx: RRR-α-toc 800–1200 IU/day
  • High-dose risks: >400 IU/day → ↑mortality (Miller), ↑prostate cancer (SELECT), ↑haemorrhagic stroke
  • Drug interactions: Warfarin (↑INR), statins+niacin (↓HDL₂), antiplatelets (bleeding)
  • Preterm: 10–20 IU/kg/day prevents haemolytic anaemia

15. 15. MCQs (10)

  1. Most biologically active vitamin E isoform:

A. γ-tocopherol

B. α-tocopherol

C. δ-tocotrienol

D. β-tocopherol

  1. Protein mediating hepatic α-tocopherol secretion into VLDL:

A. ApoB

B. α-TTP (α-tocopherol transfer protein)

C. CETP

D. LPL

  1. Antioxidant mechanism of vitamin E:

A. Scavenges OH• directly

B. Chain-breaking: LOO• + α-TOH → LOOH + α-TO•

C. Regenerates vitamin C

D. Inhibits NADPH oxidase

  1. Vitamin E deficiency causes:

A. Adequate dietary intake

B. Fat malabsorption (CF, cholestasis, abetalipoproteinaemia, AVED)

C. Excessive sun exposure

D. High PUFA diet alone

  1. Neurological triad of vitamin E deficiency:

A. Dementia, psychosis, seizures

B. Spinocerebellar ataxia, peripheral neuropathy, pigmented retinopathy

C. Myopathy, encephalopathy, optic atrophy

D. Parkinsonism, chorea, dementia

  1. AVED (Isolated Vitamin E Deficiency) genetic defect:

A. MTTP mutation

B. TTPA mutation (α-tocopherol transfer protein)

C. ApoB mutation

C. CYP4F2 mutation

  1. Abetalipoproteinaemia vs AVED key difference:

A. AVED has low cholesterol

B. Abetalipo: absent apoB, low lipids; AVED: normal lipids

C. AVED presents in infancy

D. Abetalipo responds to low-dose vit E

  1. SELECT trial outcome for vitamin E:

A. ↓Prostate cancer

B. ↑Prostate cancer (HR 1.17)

C. No effect on cancer

D. ↑Lung cancer only

  1. Meta-analysis (Miller 2005 / Cochrane 2018) vitamin E ≥400 IU/day:

A. ↓All-cause mortality

B. ↑All-cause mortality (RR ~1.03–1.04)

C. No effect on mortality

D. ↓Cardiovascular mortality only

  1. Vitamin E interaction with warfarin:

A. ↓INR

B. ↑INR (increased bleeding risk)

C. No interaction

D. Only with high-dose synthetic

16. 16. SBA Questions (5)

  1. A 6-year-old boy with cystic fibrosis presents with progressive gait ataxia, loss of vibration sense, and night blindness. Plasma α-tocopherol 1.5 µmol/L (ref 11–37). Best management?

A. Dietary counselling only

B. Water-miscible vitamin E 400 IU/day; titrate to plasma α-tocopherol >20 µmol/L

C. Intramuscular vitamin E 100 IU weekly

D. High-dose oral RRR-α-tocopherol 1200 IU/day

E. Refer for gene therapy

  1. A 14-year-old girl presents with progressive ataxia, areflexia, and retinopathy. Lipid profile normal. Plasma α-tocopherol 0.6 µmol/L. Genetic testing confirms TTPA mutation. Treatment?

A. Vitamin E 100 IU/day

B. RRR-α-tocopherol 800–1200 IU/day lifelong

C. Intramuscular vitamin E monthly

D. Dietary modification only

E. Coenzyme Q10 supplementation

  1. A 28-week preterm infant develops haemolytic anaemia (Hb 7 g/dL, reticulocytes 15%, bilirubin 8 mg/dL). Vitamin E level low. Prevention in NICU?

A. Iron supplementation

B. Vitamin E 10–20 IU/kg/day in TPN/formula

C. Exchange transfusion

D. Erythropoietin

E. N-acetylcysteine

  1. A 65-year-old man on warfarin for atrial fibrillation starts vitamin E 400 IU/day for "heart health." INR rises from 2.5 to 4.2. Mechanism?

A. Vitamin E inhibits vitamin K epoxide reductase

B. Vitamin E has antiplatelet effect + may potentiate warfarin

C. Vitamin E induces CYP2C9

D. Vitamin E displaces warfarin from albumin

E. Vitamin E increases warfarin absorption

  1. A 55-year-old man on simvastatin 40 mg + extended-release niacin 2 g for low HDL-C considers adding vitamin E 400 IU. Why should this be avoided?

A. Vitamin E causes statin myopathy

B. Vitamin E + vitamin C + β-carotene blunt the HDL₂ rise from niacin-statin combination

C. Vitamin E increases statin hepatotoxicity

D. Vitamin E causes niacin flushing

E. No interaction; safe to add

17. 17. Flashcards

  • Q: 8 vitamin E isoforms

A: 4 tocopherols (α,β,γ,δ) + 4 tocotrienols (α,β,γ,δ)

  • Q: α-TTP function

A: Hepatic protein; binds α-tocopherol preferentially → incorporates into VLDL for plasma distribution

  • Q: Antioxidant mechanism

A: LOO• + α-TOH → LOOH + α-TO• (tocopheroxyl radical); α-TO• + Vit C/GSH → α-TOH (recycled)

  • Q: Deficiency ONLY in

A: Fat malabsorption syndromes (CF, cholestasis, abetalipo, AVED, preterm)

  • Q: Neuro triad

A: Spinocerebellar ataxia, Peripheral neuropathy (axonal sensory), Pigmented retinopathy

  • Q: Abetalipoproteinaemia

A: MTTP mutation → no MTP → no chylomicron/VLDL → absent apoB, LDL, VLDL; acanthocytes, retinitis, coagulopathy

  • Q: AVED (TTPA)

A: Normal lipids/lipoproteins; plasma α-toc <5% normal; Tx: RRR-α-toc 800–1200 IU/day lifelong

  • Q: Preterm haemolytic anaemia

A: Low vit E stores + high O₂ + iron → lipid peroxidation of RBC membranes; Tx: 10–20 IU/kg/day

  • Q: SELECT trial

A: 35,533 men; vit E 400 IU/day + Se 200 µg → ↑prostate cancer HR 1.17; stopped early

  • Q: Meta-analysis >400 IU/day

A: ↑All-cause mortality (RR 1.03–1.04); no CVD benefit (Cochrane 2018, 78 RCTs, 299k)

  • Q: Warfarin interaction

A: ↑INR/bleeding risk; antiplatelet effect (↓TXA₂, ↑PGI₂)

  • Q: Statins + Niacin + Antioxidants

A: ARBITER/HATS: vit E/C/β-carotene blunt HDL₂ rise from niacin+statin

  • Q: UL & bioactivity

A: UL 1000 mg (1500 IU natural); RRR-α-toc 2x bioactivity of all-rac

  • Q: γ-tocopherol

A: Major dietary form (soybean/corn oil); traps RNS (NO₂•, ONOO⁻); anti-inflammatory

  • Q: Synthetic vs natural

A: All-rac (dl-α-toc) = 8 stereoisomers; only 1/8 is RRR; 2x dose needed for same bioactivity

18. 18. Answer Key with Explanations

1. MCQs

  1. B — α-tocopherol has highest biological activity and is preferentially retained by α-TTP.
  2. B — α-TTP (TTPA gene product) binds α-tocopherol in hepatocyte → transfers to VLDL for secretion.
  3. B — Vitamin E is a chain-breaking antioxidant: donates H to lipid peroxyl radical (LOO•), forming stable tocopheroxyl radical; recycled by vit C/GSH.
  4. B — Deficiency essentially only occurs with fat malabsorption; dietary deficiency alone extremely rare.
  5. B — Classic triad: spinocerebellar ataxia, peripheral neuropathy (axonal, sensory), pigmented retinopathy.
  6. B — AVED = TTPA mutation → α-TTP defect; MTTP = abetalipoproteinaemia.
  7. B — Abetalipoproteinaemia: MTTP mutation → no apoB → absent chylomicrons/VLDL → very low cholesterol/triglycerides; AVED: normal lipids.
  8. B — SELECT: vit E 400 IU/day + Se 200 µg → ↑prostate cancer HR 1.17 (99% CI 1.004–1.36); stopped early.
  9. B — Miller 2005 (19 RCTs, 136k) & Cochrane 2018 (78 RCTs, 299k): vit E ≥400 IU/day → ↑all-cause mortality RR 1.03–1.04.
  10. B — Vit E has antiplatelet effect (↓TXA₂, ↑PGI₂) → potentiates warfarin → ↑INR, bleeding risk.

2. SBAs

  1. B — CF-related vit E deficiency: water-miscible preparation 400 IU/day, monitor plasma α-tocopherol target >20 µmol/L; pancreatic enzymes + fat-soluble vitamin supplementation.
  2. B — AVED (TTPA confirmed): high-dose RRR-α-tocopherol 800–1200 IU/day lifelong; normal lipids differentiate from abetalipo.
  3. B — Preterm <32w/≤1500g: vit E 10–20 IU/kg/day in TPN/formula prevents haemolytic anaemia.
  4. B — Antiplatelet effect (↓TXA₂, ↑PGI₂) → ↓platelet aggregation → potentiates warfarin anticoagulation → ↑INR/bleeding.
  5. B — ARBITER/HATS: antioxidant vitamins (E, C, β-carotene) blunt niacin-induced HDL₂ rise when added to statin therapy.

19. 19. Summary

Vitamin E is a Must Know 🔴 topic for FCPS/MRCP.

Key takeaway: 8 isoforms; α-tocopherol biologically active (α-TTP retention); chain-breaking antioxidant (LOO• + α-TOH → recycled by vit C/GSH); deficiency ONLY in fat malabsorption (CF, cholestasis, abetalipoproteinaemia, AVED, preterm); neurological triad (ataxia, neuropathy, retinopathy); abetalipoproteinaemia (MTTP, no apoB) vs AVED (TTPA, normal lipids); AVED treated with high-dose RRR-α-tocopherol 800–1200 IU/day. High-dose (>400 IU) HARMFUL: Meta-analyses ↑all-cause mortality, SELECT ↑prostate cancer, ↑haemorrhagic stroke. Drug interactions: warfarin (↑INR), statins+niacin (blunts HDL₂), antiplatelets.

Exam focus: Isoforms, α-TTP, antioxidant mechanism, deficiency triad, abetalipo vs AVED, high-dose risks, SELECT trial, drug interactions.

Clinical relevance: CF/cholestasis vit E supplementation; AVED diagnosis/treatment; avoiding unnecessary vit E supplementation; warfarin monitoring.

Template version: 1.0 | Davidson 24e Ch 22 aligned | FCPS/MRCP oriented

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 5.2.04 Vitamin K Coagulation Bone Metabolism

Related: [[Nutritional Factors in Disease MOC]], [[Davidson Chapter 22 - Nutritional Factors in Disease Hierarchy]], [[../00_Index/Medicine MOC|Medicine MOC]]

[!important]
Vitamin K = K1 (phylloquinone, plants) + K2 (menaquinones MK-4 to MK-13, bacterial/animal); γ-glutamyl carboxylase cofactor → Gla residues on clotting factors II, VII, IX, X + proteins C, S; warfarin antagonises VKORC1; deficiency = bleeding (↑PT/INR); bone: osteocalcin/MGP carboxylation.

1. 1. Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Distinguish vitamin K1 (phylloquinone), K2 (menaquinones MK-n), K3 (menadione) sources and bioavailability
  • [ ] Explain γ-glutamyl carboxylase mechanism: Glu → Gla (Ca²⁺-binding); VKORC1 recycles vitamin K epoxide → hydroquinone
  • [ ] Describe warfarin mechanism: inhibits VKORC1 → ↓γ-carboxylation → dysfunctional clotting factors (↑PT/INR first, factor VII t½ 6h)
  • [ ] Identify vitamin K deficiency causes: neonates (HDN), malabsorption, broad-spectrum antibiotics, warfarin
  • [ ] State HDN prophylaxis: 1 mg phytomenadione IM at birth; treatment: 1 mg IV/PO
  • [ ] Outline bone role: osteocalcin (bone formation marker), matrix Gla protein (MGP, inhibits vascular calcification)
  • [ ] Compare MK-7 (natto) vs K1: longer half-life, better extrahepatic distribution, bone benefit
  • [ ] Recognise coumarin resistance: VKORC1 mutation; superwarfarins (brodifacoum) long half-life

2. 2. Definitions / Key Concepts

TermDefinition
Phylloquinone (K1)Plant form (green leafy veg, plant oils); major dietary source (~90% intake); side chain: phytyl (saturated)
Menaquinones (K2, MK-n)Bacterial/animal forms; side chain: unsaturated isoprenoid (n repeats); MK-4 (animal tissues), MK-7 (natto, fermented foods)
Menadione (K3)Synthetic naphthoquinone; no side chain; water-soluble; toxic (haemolysis, kernicterus) — not used therapeutically
γ-Glutamyl Carboxylase (GGCX)Vitamin K-dependent enzyme; carboxylates Glu → γ-carboxyglutamate (Gla) on target proteins; requires CO₂, O₂, vitamin K hydroquinone
Gla Residueγ-carboxyglutamic acid; binds Ca²⁺ via dicarboxylic acid; essential for protein function (clotting, bone, vascular)
VKORC1Vitamin K epoxide reductase complex subunit 1; recycles vitamin K epoxide → quinone → hydroquinone (active cofactor); warfarin target
Vitamin K CycleKH₂ (hydroquinone) → GGCX (Glu→Gla) → KO (epoxide) → VKORC1 (KO→KH₂) → repeat
PhytomenadioneSynthetic K1 analogue; used for prophylaxis/treatment (IM, IV, PO); Konakion MM (mixed micellar) for IV
HDN (Haemorrhagic Disease of Newborn)Classical (day 1–7), Late (2–12w, exclusively breastfed, cholestasis); prevented by 1 mg IM phytomenadione at birth
Osteocalcin (Bone Gla Protein, BGP)Osteoblast product; γ-carboxylated (3 Gla); binds hydroxyapatite; serum = bone formation marker; uncarboxylated (ucOC) = vit K status
Matrix Gla Protein (MGP)Vascular smooth muscle/chondrocyte product; γ-carboxylated (5 Gla); inhibits vascular calcification (binds Ca crystals); MGP-knockout → arterial calcification
Protein C & SVitamin K-dependent anticoagulants; Protein C (activated by thrombomodulin-thrombin) → inactivates Va/VIIa; Protein S = cofactor
PT/INRProthrombin time; extrinsic pathway (factor VII); most sensitive to vit K deficiency (t½ 6h); INR standardised
aPTTActivated partial thromboplastin time; intrinsic pathway; normal in early vit K deficiency, prolonged if severe
PIVKA-IIProtein Induced by Vitamin K Absence-II (uncarboxylated prothrombin); sensitive marker of vit K deficiency
SuperwarfarinsBrodifacoum, bromadiolone, difenacoum; long-acting rodenticides; inhibit VKORC1 irreversibly; half-life days–weeks; treatment: high-dose phytomenadione weeks–months
Coumarin ResistanceVKORC1 mutation (e.g., VKORC1 -1639G>A) → reduced warfarin sensitivity; requires higher dose

3. 3. Core Content

1. Section 1: Forms, Sources & Absorption

Vitamin K1 (Phylloquinone): Green leafy vegetables (spinach, kale, broccoli, Brussels sprouts), plant oils (soybean, canola). Contributes ~90% dietary intake. Bioavailability: 5–10% from vegetables (matrix-bound), higher with fat/mechanical disruption. Absorption: mixed micelles → enterocyte → chylomicron.

Vitamin K2 (Menaquinones MK-n): Bacterial synthesis (gut microbiota: MK-6 to MK-13), animal tissues (MK-4 from menadione in feed), fermented foods (natto = MK-7, cheese = MK-8/MK-9). MK-4 in meat, dairy, eggs. MK-7 (natto) — long half-life (72h vs K1 1–2h), better extrahepatic distribution.

Menadione (K3): Synthetic, water-soluble; converts to MK-4 in tissues; toxic (haemolysis, kernicterus in neonates) — NOT used clinically.

Absorption: Requires bile salts, pancreatic lipase; chylomicron secretion; lymphatic transport. K1: hepatic uptake via chylomicron remnant; MK-7: longer circulation, extrahepatic delivery.

2. Section 2: γ-Glutamyl Carboxylase & Vitamin K Cycle

Reaction: Glu (target protein) + CO₂ + O₂ + KH₂ (vitamin K hydroquinone) → Gla + KO (vitamin K epoxide) + H₂O.

Enzyme: γ-glutamyl carboxylase (GGCX); ER membrane-bound; processive (multiple Glu per substrate).

Vitamin K Cycle:

  1. KH₂ (hydroquinone) = active cofactor for GGCX
  2. GGCX uses KH₂ → carboxylates Glu → Gla → produces KO (epoxide)
  3. VKORC1 reduces KO → K (quinone) → KH₂ (hydroquinone) using NAD(P)H
  4. Warfarin inhibits VKORC1 → KO accumulates → KH₂ depleted → ↓γ-carboxylation

Target proteins (17 human Gla-proteins):

  • Coagulation: Factors II (prothrombin, 10 Gla), VII (10 Gla), IX (12 Gla), X (11 Gla)
  • Anticoagulation: Protein C (9 Gla), Protein S (11 Gla), Protein Z
  • Bone: Osteocalcin (BGP, 3 Gla), Matrix Gla Protein (MGP, 5 Gla)
  • Other: Gas6, PRGP1/2, TMG3/4, periostin

3. Section 3: Warfarin Pharmacology

Mechanism: Competitive inhibitor of VKORC1 (binds epoxide binding site); prevents KO → KH₂ recycling.

Onset: Delayed (factor half-lives): Factor VII t½ 6h (PT ↑ first, 12–24h), IX t½ 24h, X t½ 40h, II t½ 60h; full effect 3–5 days.

Monitoring: INR (target therapeutic range: 2.0–3.0 most indications, 2.5–3.5 mechanical mitral valve).

Dosing: Genetic (CYP2C9 2/3 poor metaboliser, VKORC1 -1639G>A low expression), age, weight, diet, interacting drugs.

Reversal: Vitamin K1 (phytomenadione) 1–10 mg IV/PO (IV: faster, anaphylaxis risk <0.1%); PCC (4-factor) for urgent; FFP if PCC unavailable.

Interactions:

  • ↑INR: Antibiotics (↓gut flora K2), amiodarone, metronidazole, TMP-SMX azoles, PPIs, cranberry, NSAIDs
  • ↓INR: Enzyme inducers (rifampicin, carbamazepine, phenytoin, barbiturates, St John's wort), vitamin K-rich foods

4. Section 4: Vitamin K Deficiency

Adults (rare):

  • Malabsorption: Coeliac, Crohn's, CF, biliary obstruction, short bowel, bariatric
  • Antibiotics: Broad-spectrum (3rd gen cephalosporins, carbapenems) → ↓gut flora MK synthesis
  • Warfarin: Intentional iatrogenic deficiency
  • TPN without vitamin K: Weeks without supplementation
  • Severe malnutrition

Neonates (HDN):

  • Physiological: Low placental transfer, sterile gut, low breast milk K1 (1–4 µg/L vs formula 50–100 µg/L)
  • Classical HDN: Day 1–7; bleeding (umbilical, GI, circumcision, intracranial)
  • Late HDN: 2–12 weeks; exclusively breastfed; cholestasis, malabsorption, antibiotics; intracranial haemorrhage 30–50%
  • Prevention: 1 mg phytomenadione IM at birth (universal); oral alternative: 2 mg at birth, 2 mg day 4–7, 2 mg week 4 (less reliable)

Clinical features: Bleeding (ecchymosis, haematuria, GI bleed, intracranial), ↑PT/INR (early), ↑aPTT (late), normal platelet count, normal fibrinogen, ↑PIVKA-II.

5. Section 5: Treatment of Deficiency

ScenarioPhytomenadione DoseRouteNotes
HDN Prophylaxis1 mgIM (single)Gold standard; Konakion MM paediatric
HDN Prophylaxis (Oral)2 mg ×3PO (birth, day 4–7, week 4)If parents refuse IM; lower compliance
Bleeding HDN / Late HDN1 mgIV/PO (IV faster)Repeat if INR not corrected; FFP/PCC if life-threatening
Warfarin Overdose (No Bleeding, INR 5–9)1–2 mgPOHold warfarin; restart lower dose when INR therapeutic
Warfarin Overdose (Bleeding / INR >9)5–10 mgIV (slow) + PCC/FFPPCC preferred (4-factor); monitor INR q6h
Malabsorption / Cholestasis10–20 mgPO/IM weeklyWater-miscible preparation preferred
Antibiotic-associated5–10 mgPO/IVStop antibiotic if possible; supplement during course

6. Section 6: Bone & Vascular Metabolism

Osteocalcin (BGP): Osteoblast product; 3 Gla residues; γ-carboxylation → binds hydroxyapatite (Ca²⁺); serum osteocalcin = bone formation marker; uncarboxylated osteocalcin (ucOC) ↑ with vit K deficiency; total osteocalcin reflects turnover.

Matrix Gla Protein (MGP): Vascular smooth muscle cells, chondrocytes; 5 Gla; potent inhibitor of vascular calcification (binds Ca-phosphate crystals, inhibits BMP-2); MGP-knockout mice → severe arterial calcification, death by 6w.

Vitamin K & Bone Health: Observational: low K intake → ↑fracture risk, ↓BMD; RCTs: K1 1–5 mg/day or MK-4 45 mg/day (Japan) → ↓fracture (hip/vertebral); MK-7 (natto) 180 µg/day → ↓ucOC, ↑BMD.

Vascular Calcification: MGP requires γ-carboxylation to inhibit calcification; warfarin → ↓MGP carboxylation → ↑vascular calcification (animal models; human observational).

MK-7 vs K1: MK-7 (natto) longer half-life, better extrahepatic distribution (bone, vessel), more potent at carboxylating osteocalcin/MGP at lower doses.

7. Section 7: Special Forms & Analogues

CompoundUseNotes
Phytomenadione (K1)Standard prophylaxis/treatmentIM, IV (slow), PO; Konakion MM (mixed micellar) for IV
Menadione (K3)NOT usedHaemolysis, kernicterus, GSH depletion
Menaquinone-4 (MK-4)Osteoporosis (Japan, 45 mg/day)High dose; short half-life
Menaquinone-7 (MK-7)Supplements (natto, 100–200 µg)Long half-life, extrahepatic
Warfarin / Acenocoumarol / PhenprocoumonAnticoagulationVKORC1 inhibitors; genetic dosing (CYP2C9, VKORC1)
Superwarfarins (Brodifacoum)RodenticidesLong half-life (days–weeks); Tx: high-dose K1 weeks–months
VKORC1 MutationCoumarin resistance-1639G>A → reduced expression → warfarin resistance; requires high dose

8. Section 8: Drug & Dietary Interactions

InteractionEffect on Vitamin K / Anticoagulation
Broad-spectrum antibiotics↓Gut flora MK synthesis → ↓vit K → ↑INR (if on warfarin)
Cholestyramine / ColestipolBile acid sequestrants → ↓fat absorption → ↓K1 absorption
Orlistat↓Fat absorption → ↓K1 absorption; separate dosing
High vitamin K diet (kale, spinach)↑K1 intake → ↓INR (warfarin resistance); consistent intake advised
Vitamin E high-dose↑INR (antiplatelet effect + possible carboxylase inhibition)
AmiodaroneInhibits CYP2C9 + VKORC1 → ↑INR
PPI (omeprazole)↑INR (CYP2C19 inhibition?); minor
Cranberry juiceCase reports ↑INR; mechanism unclear

9. Section 9: Laboratory Assessment

TestNormalDeficiencyNotes
PT / INR11–13.5s / 0.9–1.1↑↑ (earliest, within 12–24h)Most sensitive; factor VII t½ 6h
aPTT25–35sNormal / ↑ (late)Intrinsic pathway; factors VIII, IX, XI, XII
Fibrinogen2–4 g/LNormalNot vit K-dependent
Platelets150–400 ×10⁹/LNormalNot vit K-dependent
PIVKA-II (ucProthrombin)<2 AU/mL↑↑ (sensitive, early)Specific for vit K deficiency
Osteocalcin (total)15–50 ng/mLNormal/↓Bone formation marker
ucOC (uncarboxylated osteocalcin)<20%↑↑ (vit K status marker)Ratio ucOC/total OC = vit K status
DP-ucMGPLow↑Vascular vit K status

4. 4. Clinical Correlation

ScenarioActionNotes
Newborn 3d, cephalohematoma, not given vit K at birthPhytomenadione 1 mg IV; check PT/INR, cranial USClassical HDN; intracranial bleed risk; universal IM prophylaxis prevents
6w breastfed infant, cholestasis (biliary atresia), sudden irritability, bulging fontanellePhytomenadione 1 mg IV; cranial US/CT; urgent neurosurgeryLate HDN; cholestasis → fat malabsorption → vit K deficiency; ICH high mortality
70M on warfarin (INR 2.5), starts TMP-SMX for UTI, INR 6.2 in 3 daysHold warfarin; phytomenadione 1–2 mg PO; restart warfarin lower dose when INR therapeuticAntibiotic ↓gut flora K2 → potentiate warfarin; monitor INR q2d after antibiotic start
50F, Crohn's disease, prolonged antibiotics, easy bruising, INR 3.8 (not on warfarin)Phytomenadione 10 mg PO/IM; review antibiotics; monitor PTMalabsorption + antibiotics → vit K deficiency; not anticoagulated
65M on warfarin, mechanical mitral valve (INR target 2.5–3.5), INR 5.0, no bleedingHold 1–2 doses; phytomenadione 1 mg PO; reassess INR 24hMinor over-anticoagulation; avoid excess K (rebound subtherapeutic)
45F, post-RYGB, on warfarin, INR labile despite stable doseCheck vit K intake consistency; consider MK-7 supplement 100 µg/dayMalabsorption → variable K absorption; MK-7 longer half-life, stable levels
80M, warfarin for AF, presents with large retroperitoneal haematoma, INR 8.5PCC 25–50 IU/kg + Phytomenadione 10 mg IV slow; surgical consult; ICULife-threatening bleed; PCC immediate factor replacement; K1 sustains
30M, rat poison ingestion (brodifacoum), INR >10, bleedingPhytomenadione 50–100 mg IV/PO daily ×weeks–months; monitor INRSuperwarfarin: irreversible VKORC1 inhibition, t½ days–weeks; massive K1 needed

5. 5. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- Must know: K1 (phylloquinone) vs K2 (menaquinones MK-n); γ-glutamyl carboxylase → Gla residues; VKORC1 recycles epoxide → hydroquinone; warfarin inhibits VKORC1; HDN prophylaxis 1 mg IM at birth; late HDN in breastfed/cholestasis → ICH; PT/INR first abnormal (factor VII t½ 6h); PIVKA-II sensitive marker; osteocalcin/MGP carboxylation for bone/vascular health; MK-7 longer half-life, better extrahepatic
- Common viva: Warfarin mechanism (VKORC1); why PT ↑ before aPTT; HDN types (classical vs late); reversal of warfarin overdose (PCC + vit K); coumarin resistance VKORC1; osteocalcin vs ucOC; superwarfarin management
- Exam trap: Giving IM vit K in anticoagulated patient (haematoma risk — use IV/PO); confusing factor half-lives (VII 6h, II 60h); thinking aPTT first in vit K deficiency; missing late HDN in cholestatic infant; warfarin + antibiotics = ↑INR (give K1 prophylactically? no, just monitor)

6. 6. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
aPTT rises first in vit K deficiencyPT/INR rises FIRST (factor VII t½ 6h); aPTT late (factors IX, X, II)
Factor II (prothrombin) half-life is shortestFactor VII t½ 6h is shortest; Factor II t½ 60h longest
IM vit K safe on warfarinIM CONTRAINDICATED (haematoma risk); use IV/PO
Warfarin works immediatelyDelayed 3–5 days (factor half-lives); bridge with heparin if urgent
HDN only in first weekLate HDN: 2–12 weeks (exclusive breastfeeding + cholestasis/malabsorption); ICH risk
Osteocalcin = vit K status markerTotal osteocalcin = bone formation; ucOC/total OC = vit K status
MK-7 = K1MK-7 (natto) longer half-life (72h vs 1–2h), extrahepatic, better bone carboxylation
Superwarfarin = warfarin doseSuperwarfarin: massive K1 (50–100 mg/day) for WEEKS–MONTHS; VKORC1 irreversible inhibition
Coumarin resistance = non-complianceVKORC1 mutation (-1639G>A) → true resistance; check genotype
Vitamin K reverses warfarin instantlyIV K1: 6–12h for factor synthesis; PCC for immediate reversal

7. 7. Mnemonics

  • K forms: K1 = PLANTS (phylloquinone), K2 = BACTERIA/ANIMALS (menaquinones MK-n), K3 = SYNTHETIC TOXIC (menadione)
  • γ-Carboxylase: GGCX = Glu → Gla (needs CO₂, O₂, KH₂); produces KO (epoxide)
  • VKORC1: RECYCLES KO → KH₂; WARFARIN INHIBITS VKORC1
  • Coagulation factors: 1972 = 10 Gla (X), 9 Gla (VII), 7 (IX), 2 (II) — wait: II=10, VII=10, IX=12, X=11 → Ten-Ten-Twelve-Eleven
  • Anticoagulant proteins: C & S = Coagulation inhibition (Protein C & S + Protein Z)
  • Factor half-lives: VII SIX TEN TWO = 7, 6, 24, 40, 60 (VII 6h, IX 24h, X 40h, II 60h) — shortest VII
  • HDN: CLASSICAL = Day 1–7; LATE = 2–12 weeks (breastfed + cholestasis) → ICH
  • PT first: PT = Prothrombin (factor VII) Test = extrinsic; Shortest t½
  • Warfarin reversal: PCC = Immediate (factors); K1 = Sustained (days); Bleeding = BOTH
  • Bone: Osteocalcin = Bone formation marker; ucOC = uncarboxylated = vit K status
  • MGP: Matrix Gla Protein = Major Guard against Pathologic calcification
  • Superwarfarin: BRODIFACOUM = Big dose K1, Really long (weeks-months), Oral/IV, Daily, INR monitor, For Antidote, Continue, On and Until normal, Months

8. 8. Mind Map

Vitamin K
Forms
K1 (Phylloquinone)
– Plants: leafy greens, oils
– ~90% dietary intake
– Half-life 1–2h
K2 (Menaquinones MK-n)
– MK-4: animal tissues
– MK-7: natto (fermented soy)
– MK-6–13: gut bacteria
– Half-life: MK-7 72h
K3 (Menadione)
– Synthetic, toxic, NOT used
Metabolism
Absorption
– Bile salts, pancreatic lipase
– Chylomicron → lymph
γ-Glutamyl Carboxylase (GGCX)
– Glu + CO₂ + KH₂ → Gla + KO
– Targets: II, VII, IX, X, C, S, Z, Osteocalcin, MGP
Vitamin K Cycle
– KH₂ → GGCX (Glu→Gla) → KO → VKORC1 (KO→KH₂)
VKORC1
– Recycles epoxide → hydroquinone
– Warfarin target
Warfarin
Mechanism: Inhibits VKORC1
Onset: Delayed (factor half-lives)
Half-lives: VII 6h, IX 24h, X 40h, II 60h
Monitoring: INR (target 2–3 / 2.5–3.5)
Reversal: PCC (immediate) + K1 (sustained)
Interactions: Antibiotics ↑INR; Enzyme inducers ↓INR; Diet K ↑↓INR
Deficiency
Adults
– Malabsorption (Coeliac, CF, Cholestasis)
– Antibiotics (↓gut flora K2)
– Warfarin (iatrogenic)
– TPN without K
Neonates (HDN)
– Classical: Day 1–7 (no prophylaxis)
– Late: 2–12w (breastfed + cholestasis)
– ICH risk 30–50%
Lab: ↑PT/INR (first), ↑PIVKA-II, normal aPTT (early), normal platelets/fibrinogen
Treatment
HDN Prophylaxis: 1 mg IM at birth
HDN Treatment: 1 mg IV/PO
Warfarin Overdose
– No bleed INR 5–9: 1–2 mg PO
– Bleed/INR>9: PCC + K1 5–10 mg IV
Malabsorption: 10–20 mg PO/IM weekly
Bone & Vascular
Osteocalcin (BGP): 3 Gla → hydroxyapatite binding; serum = formation marker
MGP: 5 Gla → inhibits vascular calcification
ucOC/total OC = vit K status
MK-7 (natto) better extrahepatic carboxylation
Special
Superwarfarins (Brodifacoum)
– Irreversible VKORC1 inhibition
– t½ days–weeks
– Tx: High-dose K1 50–100 mg/day ×weeks–months
Coumarin Resistance
– VKORC1 -1639G>A mutation
– Warfarin resistance → high dose needed
Drug Interactions
– Antibiotics ↑INR (↓gut flora)
– Bile sequestrants ↓absorption
– Diet K intake consistency

9. 9. -Hour Recall Prompts

  1. Vitamin K cycle: KH₂ → GGCX → Gla + KO → VKORC1 → KH₂; warfarin blocks VKORC1
  2. Target proteins: Coagulation (II, VII, IX, X), Anticoagulation (C, S, Z), Bone (Osteocalcin, MGP)
  3. Factor half-lives: VII 6h (PT first), IX 24h, X 40h, II 60h
  4. HDN: Classical day 1–7, Late 2–12w (breastfed + cholestasis) → ICH; prophylaxis 1 mg IM birth
  5. Warfarin reversal: Bleeding = PCC + K1 5–10 mg IV; No bleed INR 5–9 = K1 1–2 mg PO
  6. Bone: Osteocalcin (formation), ucOC (vit K status), MGP (vascular calcification inhibitor)
  7. MK-7 vs K1: longer t½, extrahepatic, better bone/vessel carboxylation
  8. Superwarfarin: massive K1 weeks–months; Coumarin resistance: VKORC1 mutation

10. 10. -Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall Quality (1-5)Time SpentNotes
1
7
15
30

11. 11. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴K1 vs K2 forms; γ-carboxylase/Gla mechanism; VKORC1 cycle; warfarin inhibits VKORC1; factor half-lives (VII 6h); PT/INR first; HDN prophylaxis 1 mg IM; late HDN ICH risk; warfarin reversal (PCC + K1); osteocalcin/MGP; MK-7 advantages
Should Know 🟡PIVKA-II sensitive marker; ucOC/total OC vit K status; antibiotic warfarin interaction; superwarfarin management; coumarin resistance VKORC1; MGP vascular calcification
Nice to Know 🟢MK-4 45 mg osteoporosis Japan; MK-7 natto epidemiology; vitamin K in diabetes/insulin sensitivity; Gas6/TAM receptors; VKORC1 genotyping dosing algorithms

12. 12. My Weak Points

  • [ ] Exact VKORC1-warfarin binding stoichiometry
  • [ ] MK-7 vs MK-4 clinical trial data for fracture prevention
  • [ ] ucOC assay standardisation / reference intervals

13. 13. Self-Test Scorecard

DomainScore /10Target /10
Understanding8+
Recall8+
MCQ Performance8+
SBA Performance8+
Viva Confidence8+
TOTAL40+/50

14. 14. Exam Answer Modes

1. Long Answer / Essay (20 min)

Topic: "Vitamin K: biochemistry, deficiency states, warfarin interaction, and bone/vascular roles"

  • Forms: K1 (phylloquinone, plants), K2 (menaquinones MK-n, bacteria/animal), K3 (menadione, toxic)
  • γ-Glutamyl carboxylase: Glu → Gla (Ca²⁺ binding); requires KH₂, CO₂, O₂; produces KO
  • Vitamin K cycle: KH₂ → GGCX (Glu→Gla) → KO → VKORC1 (KO→KH₂) — warfarin inhibits VKORC1
  • Targets: Coagulation (II, VII, IX, X), Anticoagulation (C, S, Z), Bone (Osteocalcin 3 Gla, MGP 5 Gla)
  • Warfarin: inhibits VKORC1 → ↓KH₂ → ↓γ-carboxylation; onset delayed by factor half-lives (VII 6h, IX 24h, X 40h, II 60h)
  • Deficiency: Adults (malabsorption, antibiotics, warfarin); Neonates HDN (classical 1–7d, late 2–12w breastfed/cholestasis → ICH)
  • Prophylaxis: 1 mg phytomenadione IM at birth; Treatment: 1 mg IV/PO day 1, 2, 14
  • Warfarin reversal: Bleed = PCC + K1 5–10 mg IV; Minor = K1 1–2 mg PO
  • Bone: Osteocalcin (formation), ucOC (vit K status), MGP (vascular calcification inhibitor)
  • MK-7: longer half-life, extrahepatic distribution, better osteocalcin/MGP carboxylation

2. Short Note (7 min)

Topic: "Haemorrhagic Disease of the Newborn (HDN)"

  • Physiology: Low placental transfer, sterile gut, low breast milk K1 (1–4 µg/L)
  • Classical HDN: Day 1–7; bleeding (umbilical, GI, circumcision, intracranial); prevented by IM prophylaxis
  • Late HDN: 2–12 weeks; exclusively breastfed + cholestasis/malabsorption/antibiotics; ICH 30–50% mortality
  • Prophylaxis: 1 mg phytomenadione IM at birth (universal); Oral alternative: 2 mg birth, day 4–7, week 4 (less reliable)
  • Treatment: 1 mg phytomenadione IV/PO; repeat if INR not corrected; FFP/PCC if life-threatening bleeding

3. Viva Answer (3 min)

Q: "Why does PT/INR rise before aPTT in vitamin K deficiency?"

"A: Factor VII has the shortest half-life (6 hours) among vitamin K-dependent factors. PT tests the extrinsic pathway (factor VII). aPTT tests intrinsic pathway (factors VIII, IX, XI, XII) — factor IX t½ 24h, factor X t½ 40h, factor II t½ 60h. Therefore PT/INR prolongs within 12–24h of warfarin/deficiency, aPTT only later when factors IX, X, II fall."

4. Ward Case Discussion (5 min)

Case: 4w exclusively breastfed infant, biliary atresia (acholic stools, jaundice), sudden seizure, bulging fontanelle, INR 4.5.

"Diagnosis: Late HDN (breastfed + cholestasis → fat malabsorption → vit K deficiency → ICH). Immediate: Phytomenadione 1 mg IV (IM contraindicated in coagulopathy); cranial CT for ICH; neurosurgery if surgical lesion; FFP/PCC if active bleeding; water-miscible vit K 5 mg PO daily ongoing; biliary diversion surgery (Kasai) for definitive treatment."

5. Last-Night-Before-Exam Sheet (1 min)

  • Forms: K1 (phylloquinone, plants), K2 (menaquinones MK-n, bacteria/animal), K3 (toxic)
  • Cycle: KH₂ → GGCX (Glu→Gla) → KO → VKORC1 (recycles) → KH₂; Warfarin inhibits VKORC1
  • Targets: II, VII, IX, X (coag); C, S, Z (anticoag); Osteocalcin, MGP (bone/vascular)
  • Factor t½: VII 6h (PT first), IX 24h, X 40h, II 60h
  • HDN: Classical day 1–7; Late 2–12w breastfed+cholestasis → ICH; Prophylaxis 1 mg IM birth
  • Warfarin reversal: Bleed = PCC + K1 5–10 mg IV; Minor = K1 1–2 mg PO
  • Bone: Osteocalcin (formation), ucOC (vit K status), MGP (vascular calcification inhibitor)
  • MK-7 (natto): t½ 72h, extrahepatic, better bone/vessel carboxylation
  • Superwarfarin: K1 50–100 mg/day weeks–months; Coumarin resistance: VKORC1 mutation

15. 15. MCQs (10)

  1. Active cofactor for γ-glutamyl carboxylase:

A. Vitamin K quinone

B. Vitamin K epoxide

C. Vitamin K hydroquinone (KH₂)

D. Menadione

  1. Vitamin K-dependent clotting factor with SHORTEST half-life:

A. Factor II

B. Factor IX

C. Factor X

D. Factor VII (6h)

  1. Warfarin inhibits:

A. γ-Glutamyl carboxylase

B. VKORC1 (vitamin K epoxide reductase)

C. Factor Xa

D. Vitamin K absorption

  1. Late HDN typically occurs in:

A. Formula-fed infants at 3 days

B. Exclusively breastfed infants 2–12 weeks with cholestasis

C. Preterm infants on TPN

D. Infants given IM vit K at birth

  1. First coagulation test to become abnormal in vitamin K deficiency/warfarin:

A. aPTT

B. PT / INR

C. Fibrinogen

D. Platelet count

  1. Sensitive marker of vitamin K deficiency (uncarboxylated prothrombin):

A. Protein C

B. PIVKA-II

C. Osteocalcin

D. D-dimer

  1. HDN prophylaxis (universal) at birth:

A. 2 mg PO phytomenadione

B. 1 mg IM phytomenadione

C. 0.5 mg IV phytomenadione

D. 5 mg PO phytomenadione

  1. Warfarin overdose with major bleeding — immediate reversal:

A. Phytomenadione 10 mg PO alone

B. 4-factor PCC + Phytomenadione 5–10 mg IV

C. FFP alone

D. Vitamin K 1 mg IM

  1. Bone protein requiring γ-carboxylation to inhibit vascular calcification:

A. Osteocalcin

B. Matrix Gla Protein (MGP)

C. Osteopontin

D. Bone sialoprotein

  1. Menaquinone-7 (MK-7) vs Phylloquinone (K1):

A. Shorter half-life

B. Longer half-life (72h), better extrahepatic distribution

C. Lower bioavailability

D. Synthetic form only

16. 16. SBA Questions (5)

  1. A 3-week-old exclusively breastfed infant with biliary atresia presents with intracerebral haemorrhage. INR 5.2. Not given vitamin K at birth. Immediate management?

A. Fresh frozen plasma only

B. Phytomenadione 1 mg IV + cranial imaging + neurosurgical consultation

C. Phytomenadione 1 mg IM

D. Vitamin K 2 mg PO

E. Recombinant factor VIIa only

  1. A 70-year-old man on warfarin for atrial fibrillation (INR target 2–3) starts clarithromycin for pneumonia. On day 4, INR is 5.8. He has no bleeding. Best action?

A. Stop warfarin, give phytomenadione 5 mg IV

B. Hold warfarin 1–2 doses, give phytomenadione 1–2 mg PO, monitor INR daily

C. Continue warfarin same dose, increase INR monitoring

D. Switch to apixaban immediately

E. Give phytomenadione 10 mg IV

  1. A 65-year-old woman on warfarin (mechanical mitral valve, INR target 2.5–3.5) presents with large retroperitoneal haematoma. INR 8.2. Most appropriate immediate reversal?

A. Phytomenadione 10 mg IV alone

B. 4-factor PCC 25–50 IU/kg + phytomenadione 10 mg IV

C. Fresh frozen plasma 15 mL/kg + phytomenadione 5 mg PO

D. Recombinant factor VIIa 90 µg/kg

E. Stop warfarin, monitor INR

  1. An 80-year-old man with Crohn's disease on prolonged broad-spectrum antibiotics develops bruising and haematuria. Not on anticoagulants. PT 22s (control 12s), aPTT normal, platelets normal. Diagnosis?

A. Acquired haemophilia

B. Vitamin K deficiency (malabsorption + antibiotic suppression of gut flora)

C. DIC

D. Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura

E. Liver failure

  1. A 45-year-old man ingests brodifacoum (superwarfarin rodenticide). INR >10 with bleeding. Duration of high-dose vitamin K therapy required?

A. 24–48 hours

B. 5–7 days

C. Weeks to months (50–100 mg/day)

D. 2–3 weeks

E. Lifelong

17. 17. Flashcards

  • Q: γ-glutamyl carboxylase cofactor

A: Vitamin K hydroquinone (KH₂)

  • Q: Warfarin target

A: VKORC1 (vitamin K epoxide reductase)

  • Q: Factor half-lives

A: VII 6h, IX 24h, X 40h, II 60h → PT rises first

  • Q: PT vs aPTT in vit K deficiency

A: PT/INR ↑ FIRST (extrinsic, VII); aPTT ↑ LATE (intrinsic, IX, X, II)

  • Q: HDN types

A: Classical: day 1–7; Late: 2–12w (breastfed + cholestasis) → ICH risk

  • Q: HDN prophylaxis

A: 1 mg phytomenadione IM at birth (universal)

  • Q: Warfarin reversal

A: Bleed: PCC + K1 5–10 mg IV; No bleed INR 5–9: K1 1–2 mg PO

  • Q: PIVKA-II

A: Uncarboxylated prothrombin; sensitive vit K deficiency marker

  • Q: Bone proteins

A: Osteocalcin (3 Gla, formation marker); MGP (5 Gla, inhibits vascular calcification)

  • Q: ucOC

A: Uncarboxylated osteocalcin; ucOC/total OC ratio = vit K status

  • Q: MK-7 advantages

A: Half-life 72h (vs K1 1–2h); extrahepatic; better osteocalcin/MGP carboxylation

  • Q: Superwarfarin

A: Brodifacoum; irreversible VKORC1 inhibition; Tx: K1 50–100 mg/day ×weeks–months

  • Q: Coumarin resistance

A: VKORC1 mutation (-1639G>A); warfarin resistance; high-dose warfarin needed

  • Q: Bone vit K status

A: ucOC/total OC ratio; DP-ucMGP for vascular status

18. 18. Answer Key with Explanations

1. MCQs

  1. C — γ-glutamyl carboxylase uses vitamin K hydroquinone (KH₂) as cofactor; produces vitamin K epoxide (KO).
  2. D — Factor VII t½ 6h (shortest); PT/INR rises first.
  3. B — Warfarin inhibits VKORC1, preventing KO → KH₂ recycling.
  4. B — Late HDN: exclusively breastfed, 2–12 weeks, cholestasis/malabsorption → ICH.
  5. B — PT/INR (extrinsic pathway, factor VII) rises first due to shortest half-life.
  6. B — PIVKA-II = Protein Induced by Vitamin K Absence-II (uncarboxylated prothrombin); early sensitive marker.
  7. B — Universal prophylaxis: 1 mg phytomenadione IM at birth.
  8. B — Life-threatening bleed: PCC (immediate factor replacement) + phytomenadione 5–10 mg IV (sustained).
  9. B — MGP (Matrix Gla Protein) 5 Gla residues; inhibits vascular calcification; osteocalcin = bone formation.
  10. B — MK-7 (natto) half-life 72h vs K1 1–2h; better extrahepatic distribution for bone/vessel.

2. SBAs

  1. B — Late HDN + ICH: immediate IV K1 (IM contraindicated in coagulopathy), imaging, neurosurgery, FFP/PCC if bleeding.
  2. B — Clarithromycin inhibits CYP2C9/VKORC1 → ↑INR; minor over-anticoagulation (INR 5.8, no bleed): hold warfarin 1–2 doses, low-dose PO K1, daily INR.
  3. B — Mechanical valve + life-threatening bleed: PCC (immediate) + K1 10 mg IV (sustained); target INR correction rapid.
  4. B — Crohn's + antibiotics → fat malabsorption + ↓gut flora K2 → vit K deficiency; not on anticoagulants; normal aPTT/platelets fits.
  5. C — Superwarfarin (brodifacoum): irreversible VKORC1 inhibition, half-life weeks; requires massive K1 50–100 mg/day for weeks–months.

19. 19. Summary

Vitamin K is a Must Know 🔴 topic for FCPS/MRCP.

Key takeaway: K1 (phylloquinone, plants), K2 (menaquinones MK-n, bacteria/animal); γ-carboxylase uses KH₂ → Glu→Gla (Ca²⁺ binding); VKORC1 recycles KO→KH₂; warfarin inhibits VKORC1. Factor VII t½ 6h → PT/INR first. HDN: classical day 1–7, late 2–12w (breastfed+cholestasis) → ICH; prophylaxis 1 mg IM birth. Warfarin reversal: bleed = PCC + K1 IV; minor = K1 PO. Bone: osteocalcin (formation), ucOC (vit K status), MGP (vascular calcification inhibitor). MK-7 longer half-life, extrahepatic. Superwarfarin: massive K1 weeks–months.

Exam focus: γ-carboxylase/VKORC1 cycle, factor half-lives, PT vs aPTT, HDN types/prophylaxis, warfarin reversal, PIVKA-II, osteocalcin/MGP, MK-7 vs K1.

Clinical relevance: Universal HDN prophylaxis; warfarin management (INR monitoring, reversal, interactions); bone health (MK-7); antibiotic-warfarin interaction; superwarfarin poisoning.

Template version: 1.0 | Davidson 24e Ch 22 aligned | FCPS/MRCP oriented

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 5.3 Water-Soluble Vitamins 6
▸ 5.3.01 Folate B9 Anaemia Neural Tube Defects

Related: [[Nutritional Factors in Disease MOC]], [[Davidson Chapter 22 - Nutritional Factors in Disease Hierarchy]], [[../00_Index/Medicine MOC|Medicine MOC]]

[!important]
Folate (B9) = 1-carbon transfer (DNA synthesis, methylation, AA metabolism); cofactor for thymidylate synthase (dUMP→dTMP); deficiency = megaloblastic anaemia, neutropenia, hypersegmented neutrophils, neural tube defects; methotrexate, trimethoprim, OCP, alcohol deplete; mandatory pre-conception 400 µg/day.

1. 1. Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Describe folate forms (folic acid, dihydrofolate DHF, tetrahydrofolate THF, 5,10-methylene-THF, 5-methyl-THF), absorption (jejunum), enterohepatic circulation
  • [ ] Explain 1-carbon transfer: serine → glycine (5,10-methylene-THF), dUMP → dTMP (thymidylate synthase), homocysteine → methionine (5-methyl-THF + B12)
  • [ ] List deficiency causes: poor diet, malabsorption, drugs (MTX, trimethoprim, OCP, anticonvulsants), alcohol, ↑demand (pregnancy, haemolysis, dialysis)
  • [ ] Recognise deficiency: megaloblastic anaemia (macrocytic, MCV >100), hypersegmented neutrophils (5+ lobes), pancytopenia, glossitis, NTD
  • [ ] State NTD prevention: 400 µg folic acid/day preconception + 1st trimester; 5 mg/day in previous NTD or on antiepileptics
  • [ ] Differentiate folate from B12 deficiency: NORMAL methylmalonic acid in folate; NORMAL neurological in folate (vs subacute combined degeneration in B12)
  • [ ] Treatment: oral folic acid 5 mg/day ×4 months; parenteral if malabsorption

2. 2. Definitions / Key Concepts

TermDefinition
Folate (B9)Water-soluble; generic term for vitamer family (folic acid, DHF, THF, 5-methyl-THF, etc.)
Folic AcidSynthetic, oxidised form (in supplements, fortified foods); fully bioavailable; converted to THF by DHFR
Tetrahydrofolate (THF)Reduced active form; 1-carbon carrier at oxidation states (-COOH, -CHO, -CH₃) attached to N⁵, N¹⁰
5,10-Methylene-THFCoenzyme for thymidylate synthase (dUMP + N⁵,N¹⁰-CH₂-THF → dTMP + DHF)
5-Methyl-THFMajor circulating form; 1-carbon donor to homocysteine → methionine (B12-dependent methionine synthase)
Thymidylate Synthase (TS)dUMP → dTMP; BLOOD-DEPENDENT RATE LIMITING in DNA synthesis; targets 5-FU (F → 5-FdUMP binds TS)
Methionine Synthase (MS)Homocysteine + 5-methyl-THF → methionine + THF; B12 (methylcobalamin) cofactor
Methionine Synthase Reductase (MTRR)Regenerates methylcobalamin (methyl-Cbl → SAM cycle)
MTHFR (Methylenetetrahydrofolate Reductase)5,10-methylene-THF → 5-methyl-THF; FAD (B2) cofactor; C677T polymorphism (homozygous 10–15%)
FormiminotransferaseHistidine catabolism; THF + formimino-Glu → N-formimino-THF; deficiency = FIGLU excretion
Megaloblastic AnaemiaMacrocytic (MCV >100), hypersegmented neutrophils (5+ lobes), pancytopenia, ineffective erythropoiesis; B12/folate deficiency, drugs
Hypersegmented Neutrophils≥5 lobes; earliest sign of megaloblastic change; before anaemia
Megaloblastic MadnessNeuropsychiatric symptoms in severe B12/folate deficiency (more prominent in B12)
Neural Tube Defects (NTD)Anencephaly, spina bifida, encephalocoele; folate-dependent; prevented by periconception 400 µg/day
Pancytopenia↓RBC, ↓WBC, ↓platelets; megaloblastic = ALL cell lines affected (marrow hypercellular)
Folinic Acid (Leucovorin)5-formyl-THF; reduced form (bypass DHFR); used in MTX rescue, 5-FU enhancement
DHFR (Dihydrofolate Reductase)Folic acid → DHF → THF; inhibited by methotrexate, trimethoprim, pyrimethamine, proguanil

3. 3. Core Content

1. Section 1: Folate Biochemistry & 1-Carbon Transfer

Sources: Green leafy veg (spinach, asparagus, broccoli), legumes, citrus, liver, fortified grains; thermolabile (overcooking destroys 50–90%).

Absorption: Jejunum; passive diffusion + carrier-mediated (RFC, PCFT/SLC46A1); monoglutamate form in circulation.

Reduction: Folic acid → DHF (DHFR) → THF (DHFR, NADPH); 5-methyl-THF is major dietary form.

Polyglutamate storage: THF + 3–6 glutamate residues (γ-linked) → retained in cells; converted to monoglutamate for export.

Enterohepatic circulation: Daily biliary excretion (~100 µg) + intestinal reabsorption = significant source.

1-Carbon Transfer Reactions (THF = universal carrier):

ReactionTHF formFunction
dUMP → dTMP (thymidylate synthase)5,10-methylene-THFDNA synthesis
Homocysteine → methionine (methionine synthase)5-methyl-THFMethylation (SAM cycle)
Glycine ↔ serine (SHMT)5,10-methylene-THFAA interconversion
Histidine catabolism (FIGLU → glutamate)5-formimino-THFAA catabolism
Purine synthesis (C2, C8)N¹⁰-formyl-THFDNA/RNA
Methionine → SAM → methyl transferSAM (5-methyl-THF derived)Methylation (DNA, histones, neurotransmitters)

Why megaloblastic? ↓dTMP → ↓DNA synthesis → nuclear-cytoplasmic asynchrony (cytoplasm matures normally, nucleus lags) → megaloblastosis; high demand tissues (bone marrow, GI mucosa) most affected.

2. Section 2: Folate Deficiency — Causes

CauseMechanism
Poor dietary intakeElderly, alcoholics, vegan, anorexia; "tea-and-toast" diet
MalabsorptionCoeliac, Crohn's, short bowel, bariatric, achlorhydria (PCFT/SLC46A1 needs pH 5–6)
Drugs:
- Methotrexate (MTX)DHFR inhibitor; most potent (Rheumatology, Oncology)
- Trimethoprim (TMP)Bacterial DHFR (also mammalian at high dose)
- PyrimethamineDHFR (Toxoplasma, malaria)
- OCP↓folate absorption, ↑plasma clearance; OCP/HRT users ↓red cell folate
- AnticonvulsantsPhenytoin, phenobarbital, carbamazepine → ↑CYP450, ↑folate catabolism
- Methotrexate (leucovorin rescue)Cancer chemotherapy
- Sulfasalazine↓folate absorption (competitive)
Alcohol↓absorption, ↓hepatic uptake, ↑renal excretion; macrocytosis in alcoholics
↑DemandPregnancy (fetal DNA synthesis), haemolysis (sickle, spherocytosis), dialysis, prematurity
GeneticMTHFR C677T (↑homocysteine); PCFT/SLC46A1 (hereditary folate malabsorption)

3. Section 3: Clinical Features

Haematological:

  • Megaloblastic anaemia: Macrocytic (MCV >100, often 110–130), hypersegmented neutrophils (≥5 lobes, earliest sign), oval macrocytes, anisopoikilocytosis, Howell-Jolly bodies
  • Pancytopenia: ↓RBC, ↓WBC, ↓platelets (marrow hypercellular due to ineffective haematopoiesis)
  • Reticulocytopenia (low retic); intramedullary haemolysis → ↑LDH, ↑bilirubin (indirect), ↓haptoglobin

GI:

  • Glossitis (beefy red, magenta tongue)
  • Angular cheilosis, stomatitis
  • Diarrhoea, malabsorption (small bowel mucosal cells affected)

Neuropsychiatric (more prominent in B12, but can occur in severe folate):

  • Cognitive decline, memory impairment, "megaloblastic madness"
  • Peripheral neuropathy (less than B12)
  • Spina bifida / NTD in pregnancy

Pregnancy:

  • NTD (neural tube defects): Anencephaly, spina bifida, encephalocoele
  • Folate deficiency → ↓cell migration, failure of neural tube closure (days 21–28 gestation)
  • Placental abruption, recurrent miscarriage
  • Pre-eclampsia (controversial), IUGR

4. Section 4: Neural Tube Defect Prevention

NTD types: Anencephaly, spina bifida occulta, meningocoele, meningomyelocoele, encephalocoele; risk factors: previous NTD (10x risk), maternal diabetes, valproate, carbamazepine, folate deficiency.

Prevention (FSA/ACOG/RCOG guidelines):

  • All women of childbearing age: 400 µg/day folic acid
  • Periconception (1 month preconception through 12 weeks gestation): 400 µg/day minimum
  • Previous NTD or family history: 5 mg/day
  • On antiepileptics (valproate, carbamazepine, phenobarbital): 5 mg/day
  • Twins, diabetes, obesity, haemolysis: 5 mg/day

Mechanism: Folate required for DNA synthesis/cell proliferation during neurulation (21–28 days post-conception; often before pregnancy confirmed).

Fortification: Mandatory in 80+ countries (US, Canada, UK since 2021: non-wholemeal wheat flour 250 µg/100g). NTD incidence ↓20–50%.

5. Section 5: Diagnosis

TestFolate DeficiencyB12 Deficiency
Serum folate<7 nmol/L (3 µg/L)Often normal/low
Red cell folate<340 nmol/LLow
Serum B12Normal<150 pmol/L
Homocysteine↑↑ (5–20 µmol/L)↑↑ (10–50)
Methylmalonic acid (MMA)Normal↑↑ (>0.4 µmol/L)
MCV>100>100
Hypersegmented neutrophilsYesYes
Neurological signsRare (mild)Subacute combined degeneration
Response to B12 aloneNoneImproves anaemia only
Response to folate aloneImprovesHides B12 (worsens neuro)

Key point: Folate treatment in B12 deficiency improves anaemia but worsens neurological disease (irreversible). Always check B12 before folate supplementation if deficiency suspected.

6. Section 6: Treatment

ScenarioFolate DoseDuration
Deficiency (no malabsorption)5 mg/day PO4 months (cover RBC lifespan)
Malabsorption5 mg/day IV/IMUntil cause corrected
Pregnancy (prophylaxis)400 µg/day (high risk 5 mg)1 month preconception → 12w gestation
Methotrexate (rheumatology)5 mg/week (timed)Ongoing; LEUCOVORIN 15 mg 24h after MTX for toxicity
MTX cancer (rescue)Leucovorin 15 mg/m² IV/PO12–24h after MTX; repeat q6h
5-FU enhancementLeucovorin 200 mg/m² + 5-FUColorectal cancer
Haemodialysis1–5 mg/dayOngoing; remove with high-flux HD
Hereditary folate malabsorption (PCFT)Parenteral folate (5-formyl-THF)Lifelong

4. 4. Clinical Correlation

ScenarioActionNotes
35F, macrocytic anaemia MCV 108, hypersegmented neutrophilsCheck B12 first; if B12 normal, folate 5 mg/day ×4m; dietary counsellingNEVER give folate before B12
30F planning pregnancy, on OCP, healthyStart folic acid 400 µg/day 1 month before conception; continue 12wOCP depletes folate
28F, previous anencephalic foetusFolic acid 5 mg/day periconception + 1st trimesterPrevious NTD = 5 mg
50M on MTX for RA, macrocytosis, mouth ulcersFolic acid 5 mg/week 24h after MTX; monitor blood counts; consider leucovorin rescueMTX = DHFR inhibition
30F on valproate for epilepsy, planning pregnancyFolic acid 5 mg/day pre-conception; valproate is teratogenic (NTD 1–2%); consider alternative AED (lamotrigine, levetiracetam)Valproate = highest NTD risk of AEDs
70M alcoholic, macrocytosis, hypersegmented neutrophils, B12 normal, folate lowFolic acid 5 mg/day ×3m; alcohol cessation; thiamine, multivitaminMultiple B-vitamin deficiencies
6m infant, seizures, megaloblastic anaemia, ↑homocysteine, normal MMAHereditary folate malabsorption (PCFT); parenteral 5-formyl-THFPCFT/SLC46A1 mutation; pH-sensitive transporter

5. 5. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- Must know: 1-carbon transfer (dUMP→dTMP = DNA synthesis; homocysteine→methionine = methylation); megaloblastic anaemia (macrocytic, hypersegmented neutrophils); deficiency causes (poor diet, MTX, trimethoprim, OCP, alcohol, pregnancy, haemolysis); NTD prevention 400 µg/day periconception, 5 mg/day if previous NTD/on valproate; check B12 before folate; normal MMA in folate deficiency (vs ↑↑ in B12)
- Common viva: 1-carbon reactions; megaloblastic vs non-megaloblastic; B12 vs folate (MMA, neurological); periconception dosing; methotrexate rescue (leucovorin 15 mg); MTHFR C677T
- Exam trap: Giving folate before B12 (worsens neurological); confusing hypersegmented neutrophils with shift left; thinking folate treats B12 neurological; macrocytosis in alcohol without B12/folate deficiency (direct effect)

6. 6. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
Give folate for macrocytic anaemia without checking B12Check B12 first; folate hides B12 anaemia, worsens neurological
Homocysteine ↑ in both folate and B12MMA ↑ only in B12 (sensitive/specific for B12)
Folate deficiency causes NTDFolate PREVENTS NTD; deficiency increases risk; periconception 400 µg/day
Hypersegmented neutrophils = band cellsHypersegmented = ≥5 lobes (earliest sign of megaloblastosis)
Methotrexate folateMTX = DHFR inhibitor → functional folate deficiency; LEUCOVORIN (5-formyl-THF) bypasses
5 mg folate for all pregnancy400 µg for normal; 5 mg only if previous NTD/anticonvulsants/diabetes/obesity
OCP doesn't affect folateOCP ↓folate absorption + ↑plasma clearance; consider 400 µg supplement
Macrocytosis = folateMacro ≠ megaloblastic; alcohol = macrocytosis without megaloblastosis; reticulocytosis

7. 7. Mnemonics

  • 1-Carbon reactions: DTS = DNA Thymidylate Synthase (5,10-methylene-THF); MS = Methionine Synthase (5-methyl-THF + B12)
  • Folate forms: 5-10-5 = 5,10-methylene-THF (dTMP) ↔ 5-methyl-THF (methionine)
  • Megaloblastic triad: MHH = Macrocytic, Hypersegmented neutrophils, Hypercellular marrow
  • NTD prevention: 400-4-12 = 400 µg/day, 1 month before, 12 weeks gestation; 5 mg if high risk
  • Methotrexate rescue: LEUCOVORIN 15 mg = Leucovorin, Every Until, Cytes Optimal, Via Orally, Rescue Injected, Needed
  • Drug-induced folate deficiency: MS-TOP-OCP = MTX, Sulfasalazine, Trimethoprim, OCP, Pyrimethamine + Anticonvulsants
  • Folate vs B12: Hcy in BOTH; MMA ONLY in B12; NEURO only in B12
  • Megaloblastic earliest sign: Hypersegmented Neutrophils (5+ lobes) before anaemia

8. 8. Mind Map

Folate B9
Biochemistry
Forms
– Folic acid (synthetic)
– DHF, THF, 5,10-methylene-THF
– 5-methyl-THF (major circulating)
Absorption
– Jejunum, RFC, PCFT/SLC46A1 (pH 5-6)
– Polyglutamate storage
1-Carbon Transfer
– dUMP → dTMP (TS, 5,10-methylene-THF)
– Homocysteine → Methionine (MS, 5-methyl-THF, B12)
– Glycine ↔ Serine (SHMT)
– Purine synthesis (C2, C8, N10-formyl-THF)
Deficiency Causes
Diet: alcohol, elderly, vegan
Malabsorption: coeliac, CF, PCFT
Drugs: MTX, TMP, OCP, phenytoin, sulfasalazine
↑Demand: pregnancy, haemolysis, dialysis
Genetic: MTHFR C677T, PCFT mutation
Clinical Features
Haematological
– Megaloblastic anaemia (MCV >100)
– Hypersegmented neutrophils (earliest)
– Pancytopenia (hypercellular marrow)
– ↑LDH, ↑bilirubin
GI: Glossitis, diarrhoea
Pregnancy: NTD (anencephaly, spina bifida)
Neuro: "Megaloblastic madness"
NTD Prevention
Standard: 400 µg/day preconception + 1st trimester
High Risk: 5 mg/day (previous NTD, valproate, DM, obesity, haemolysis)
Mechanism: Neural tube closure days 21-28
Fortification: Mandatory in 80+ countries
Diagnosis
Serum folate <7 nmol/L
Red cell folate <340 nmol/L
Homocysteine ↑↑
MMA NORMAL
Hypersegmented neutrophils
Treatment
Deficiency: 5 mg/day ×4 months
Pregnancy: 400 µg (5 mg high risk)
MTX rescue: Leucovorin 15 mg 24h after
Malabsorption: Parenteral
B12 vs Folate
MMA: ↑ in B12 only
Neuro: Only B12
Folate alone: improves anaemia, worsens B12 neuro

9. 9. -Hour Recall Prompts

  1. 1-carbon transfer: dUMP→dTMP (5,10-methylene-THF); homocysteine→methionine (5-methyl-THF + B12)
  2. Deficiency causes: poor diet, MTX, trimethoprim, OCP, alcohol, haemolysis, pregnancy
  3. Megaloblastic anaemia: macrocytic MCV >100, hypersegmented neutrophils (earliest), pancytopenia
  4. NTD prevention: 400 µg/day standard, 5 mg if high risk; preconception 1 month
  5. Folate vs B12: MMA normal in folate (↑ in B12); neuro only in B12
  6. Methotrexate rescue: leucovorin (5-formyl-THF) 15 mg IV/PO 24h after MTX
  7. MTHFR C677T: most common folate polymorphism; ↑homocysteine
  8. OCP/HRT depletes folate; periconception 400 µg

10. 10. -Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall Quality (1-5)Time SpentNotes
1
7
15
30

11. 11. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴1-carbon transfer (dTMP, methionine); megaloblastic anaemia (macrocytic, hypersegmented neutrophils); deficiency causes (MTX, TMP, OCP, alcohol, pregnancy); NTD prevention (400 µg / 5 mg); check B12 before folate; normal MMA in folate; methotrexate rescue (leucovorin)
Should Know 🟡MTHFR C677T; MTRR mutation; OCP folate depletion; fortification programmes; valproate NTD risk; MTHFD1 polymorphisms; absorption (PCFT pH)
Nice to Know 🟢5-Formyl-THF (leucovorin) rescue protocols; polyglutamylation; methyl-trap hypothesis; folate in neurodevelopmental disorders; Hcy-cysteine remethylation genetics

12. 12. My Weak Points

  • [ ] MTHFR C677T prevalence by ethnicity
  • [ ] Methotrexate rescue dose timing detail
  • [ ] PCFT/SLC46A1 hereditary malabsorption

13. 13. Self-Test Scorecard

DomainScore /10Target /10
Understanding8+
Recall8+
MCQ Performance8+
SBA Performance8+
Viva Confidence8+
TOTAL40+/50

14. 14. Exam Answer Modes

1. Long Answer / Essay (20 min)

Topic: "Folate: biochemistry, deficiency, and clinical significance"

  • Biochemistry: 1-carbon transfer (THF cofactor); dUMP→dTMP (TS, 5,10-methylene-THF); homocysteine→methionine (MS, 5-methyl-THF, B12); purine synthesis
  • Deficiency: megaloblastic anaemia (MCV >100, hypersegmented neutrophils, pancytopenia); GI (glossitis); NTD in pregnancy
  • Causes: poor diet, alcohol, malabsorption, drugs (MTX, TMP, OCP, anticonvulsants), ↑demand
  • NTD prevention: 400 µg/day periconception; 5 mg if high risk (previous NTD, valproate, DM)
  • Diagnosis: ↓serum/red cell folate, ↑homocysteine, normal MMA; check B12 first (folate hides B12)
  • Treatment: 5 mg/day ×4 months; leucovorin rescue for MTX toxicity

2. Short Note (7 min)

Topic: "Folate vs B12 Deficiency"

  • Both: Macrocytic megaloblastic anaemia, hypersegmented neutrophils, pancytopenia, ↑homocysteine
  • B12 only: ↑MMA (sensitive/specific); subacute combined degeneration (posterior + lateral columns, peripheral neuropathy, dementia)
  • Folate only: Risk of NTD; very rare neurological
  • Critical: Folate treatment in B12 deficiency worsens neurological disease (irreversible) despite improving anaemia
  • Practical: Always check B12 (or co-prescribe B12) when starting folate for macrocytic anaemia

3. Viva Answer (3 min)

Q: "Why is folate given before conception?"

"A: Neural tube closure occurs days 21–28 post-conception — often before pregnancy confirmed. NTD (anencephaly, spina bifida) result from failed closure. Folate required for DNA synthesis/cell proliferation during neurulation. 400 µg/day from 1 month preconception through 12 weeks gestation; 5 mg if high risk (previous NTD, valproate, DM, obesity). Fortification programmes ↓NTD 20–50%."

4. Ward Case Discussion (5 min)

Case: 30F on MTX 15 mg weekly for RA, 6 months, presents with macrocytic anaemia (Hb 9, MCV 110), hypersegmented neutrophils, mouth ulcers. B12 normal.

"Diagnosis: MTX-induced functional folate deficiency (DHFR inhibition). Treatment: Folic acid 5 mg/week 24 hours after MTX (separate dose to avoid competitive inhibition). Monitor Hb, MCV. Mouth ulcers improve with folate. Alternative: leucovorin 15 mg rescue for severe toxicity."

5. Last-Night-Before-Exam Sheet (1 min)

  • 1-Carbon transfer: dUMP→dTMP (TS, 5,10-methylene-THF); Hcy→Met (MS, 5-methyl-THF, B12); purine (C2, C8)
  • Megaloblastic: Macrocytic, hypersegmented neutrophils (earliest), pancytopenia, ↑LDH
  • Causes: Diet, alcohol, malabsorption, MTX, TMP, OCP, anticonvulsants, ↑demand
  • NTD prevention: 400 µg standard / 5 mg high risk; preconception 1 month
  • B12 vs Folate: MMA ↑ in B12 only; neuro only in B12; CHECK B12 BEFORE FOLATE
  • MTX rescue: Leucovorin (5-formyl-THF) 15 mg 24h after MTX
  • OCP: ↓folate absorption + ↑plasma clearance; consider 400 µg supplement
  • Fortification: 80+ countries; ↓NTD 20–50%

15. 15. MCQs (10)

  1. Major circulating form of folate:

A. Folic acid

B. DHF

C. THF

D. 5-Methyl-THF

  1. Folate cofactor for thymidylate synthase (dUMP → dTMP):

A. 5-Methyl-THF

B. 5,10-Methylene-THF

C. N¹⁰-formyl-THF

D. THF

  1. Neural tube closure occurs:

A. Days 7–14 post-conception

B. Days 21–28 post-conception

C. Weeks 8–12 gestation

D. Trimester 2

  1. Standard folic acid dose for NTD prevention (no risk factors):

A. 100 µg/day

B. 400 µg/day

C. 1 mg/day

D. 5 mg/day

  1. Folic acid dose in women with previous NTD or on valproate:

A. 400 µg/day

B. 1 mg/day

C. 5 mg/day

D. 10 mg/day

  1. Biochemical marker elevated in BOTH folate and B12 deficiency:

A. Methylmalonic acid (MMA)

B. Homocysteine

C. Methylmalonic acid AND homocysteine

D. Neither

  1. Biochemical marker SPECIFIC for B12 deficiency:

A. Homocysteine

B. Methylmalonic acid (MMA)

C. Cystathionine

D. Methionine

  1. Methotrexate rescue agent (bypass DHFR):

A. Folic acid

B. Folinic acid (leucovorin, 5-formyl-THF)

C. 5-methyl-THF

D. Pyridoxine

  1. Drug causing folate deficiency by ↓absorption + ↑clearance:

A. Methotrexate

B. Trimethoprim

C. Oral contraceptive pill

D. Phenytoin

  1. Earliest sign of megaloblastic anaemia:

A. Macrocytosis

B. Hypersegmented neutrophils (≥5 lobes)

C. Pancytopenia

D. ↑MCV

16. 16. SBA Questions (5)

  1. A 32-year-old woman with previous anencephalic pregnancy is planning another pregnancy. What folate dose should she take periconceptionally?

A. 400 µg/day

B. 1 mg/day

C. 5 mg/day

D. 10 mg/day

E. No folate needed

  1. A 60-year-old man on methotrexate 15 mg weekly for rheumatoid arthritis develops macrocytic anaemia (Hb 9.5, MCV 105), hypersegmented neutrophils, and mouth ulcers. B12 normal. Management?

A. Stop methotrexate permanently

B. Folic acid 5 mg/week, taken 24 hours after methotrexate

C. Leucovorin 200 mg IV

D. Folic acid 5 mg daily

E. Switch to alternative DMARD

  1. A 28-year-old woman on valproate for epilepsy is planning pregnancy. Folate advice?

A. No folate needed

B. 400 µg/day throughout pregnancy

C. 5 mg/day preconception and 1st trimester; consider AED switch

D. Folic acid 10 mg daily

E. Folic acid IV

  1. A 50-year-old man with macrocytic anaemia (Hb 8.5, MCV 115), hypersegmented neutrophils. B12 is 90 pmol/L (low). Methylmalonic acid 2.5 µmol/L (very high), homocysteine 60 µmol/L. Diagnosis?

A. Folate deficiency

B. B12 deficiency

C. Combined folate + B12 deficiency

D. Anaemia of chronic disease

E. Myelodysplasia

  1. A 35-year-old woman of childbearing age with no pregnancy plan and on OCP. What dietary supplement should be considered?

A. No supplement

B. Folic acid 400 µg/day

C. Iron

D. Vitamin D

E. Vitamin C

17. 17. Flashcards

  • Q: 1-Carbon transfer: dTMP

A: 5,10-methylene-THF (thymidylate synthase, dUMP→dTMP)

  • Q: 1-Carbon transfer: Methionine

A: 5-methyl-THF (methionine synthase, Hcy→Met, B12 cofactor)

  • Q: Megaloblastic earliest sign

A: Hypersegmented neutrophils (≥5 lobes) before anaemia

  • Q: NTD prevention standard

A: 400 µg/day, 1 month preconception → 12 weeks gestation

  • Q: NTD prevention high risk

A: 5 mg/day (previous NTD, valproate, DM, obesity, haemolysis)

  • Q: Folate vs B12 MMA

A: MMA NORMAL in folate; ↑↑ in B12 (sensitive/specific)

  • Q: Folate vs B12 neuro

A: Neuro only in B12 (SCD); folate hides anaemia, worsens B12 neuro

  • Q: MTX rescue

A: Leucovorin 15 mg 24h after MTX (5-formyl-THF bypasses DHFR)

  • Q: Drugs depleting folate

A: MS-TOP-OCP = MTX, Sulfasalazine, Trimethoprim, OCP, Pyrimethamine + Anticonvulsants

  • Q: MTHFR C677T

A: Most common folate polymorphism; ↑homocysteine; risk CVD/stroke

  • Q: Folate excess effect

A: No toxicity (water-soluble); but may hide B12 deficiency (anaemia correct, neuro progresses)

18. 18. Answer Key with Explanations

1. MCQs

  1. D — 5-methyl-THF is the major circulating form; THF accepts one-carbon units at N⁵ or N¹⁰ positions.
  2. B — 5,10-methylene-THF provides carbon for dUMP → dTMP via thymidylate synthase (rate-limiting DNA synthesis step).
  3. B — Neural tube closure days 21–28 post-conception (3–4 weeks gestation); before pregnancy often confirmed; hence periconception folate.
  4. B — Standard 400 µg/day from 1 month preconception through 12 weeks gestation; 5 mg if risk factors.
  5. C — High risk (previous NTD, valproate/carbamazepine, DM, obesity): 5 mg/day.
  6. B — Homocysteine ↑ in BOTH folate and B12 deficiency; MMA ↑ ONLY in B12 (key differentiator).
  7. B — Methylmalonic acid (MMA) is sensitive and specific for B12 deficiency; accumulated due to impaired methylmalonyl-CoA mutase.
  8. B — Folinic acid (5-formyl-THF, leucovorin) is reduced form; bypasses DHFR block from methotrexate.
  9. C — OCP decreases folate absorption and increases plasma clearance; contributes to megaloblastic changes.
  10. B — Hypersegmented neutrophils (≥5 lobes) appear in blood before macrocytosis/anaemia; earliest sign.

2. SBAs

  1. C — Previous NTD = 5 mg/day preconception + 1st trimester; 10x recurrence risk.
  2. B — MTX-induced folate deficiency: folic acid 5 mg/week 24h after MTX (separate dose to avoid competitive DHFR inhibition); mouth ulcers improve.
  3. C — Valproate (highest NTD risk of AEDs) + periconception folate 5 mg/day; consider switch to lamotrigine/levetiracetam if planning pregnancy.
  4. B — Low B12 + ↑↑MMA (specific) + ↑Hcy = B12 deficiency; folate deficiency would have normal MMA.
  5. B — OCP depletes folate; 400 µg/day periconception recommended for all women of childbearing age (also covers unplanned pregnancy).

19. 19. Summary

Folate (B9) is a Must Know 🔴 topic for FCPS/MRCP.

Key takeaway: 1-carbon transfer: dUMP→dTMP (DNA synthesis via TS) + homocysteine→methionine (methylation via MS, B12 cofactor). Deficiency = megaloblastic anaemia (macrocytic, hypersegmented neutrophils), glossitis, NTD in pregnancy. Causes: diet, alcohol, MTX, trimethoprim, OCP, anticonvulsants, ↑demand (pregnancy/haemolysis). NTD prevention: 400 µg/day standard, 5 mg high risk (previous NTD, valproate, DM), periconception 1 month. Check B12 before folate (folate hides B12 anaemia, worsens B12 neuro). Methotrexate rescue: leucovorin 15 mg 24h after.

Exam focus: 1-carbon reactions, megaloblastic vs B12 (MMA, neuro), NTD prevention, MTX rescue, MTHFR, OCP effect.

Clinical relevance: Universal periconception folate; MTX monitoring; B12 check before folate; OCP counselling.

Template version: 1.0 | Davidson 24e Ch 22 aligned | FCPS/MRCP oriented

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 5.3.02 Pyridoxine B6 Biotin Pantothenic Acid

Related: [[Nutritional Factors in Disease MOC]], [[Davidson Chapter 22 - Nutritional Factors in Disease Hierarchy]], [[../00_Index/Medicine MOC|Medicine MOC]]

[!important]
Pyridoxine (B6) = PLP cofactor for 140+ enzymes (transamination, decarboxylation, glycogen phosphorylase, haem synthesis); deficiency = sideroblastic anaemia, peripheral neuropathy, seizures, cheilosis, glossitis; Biotin = carboxylase cofactor; deficiency = dermatitis, alopecia, conjunctivitis; Pantothenic acid = CoA/ACP; deficiency RARE (burning feet syndrome).

1. 1. Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Describe pyridoxine forms (PN, PM, PL) → phosphorylation → PLP (active); PLP-dependent enzyme classes
  • [ ] List pyridoxine deficiency causes: INH, penicillamine, hydralazine, oral contraceptives, alcohol, malabsorption, dialysis
  • [ ] Recognise pyridoxine deficiency: sideroblastic anaemia (↓ALAS2), peripheral neuropathy (↓GABA), seizures (infants), cheilosis, glossitis, hyperirritability
  • [ ] Explain pyridoxine toxicity: sensory neuronopathy (dorsal column) at >100 mg/day chronic; reversible on stopping
  • [ ] Describe biotin → carboxylases (ACC, PCC, MCC, PC); deficiency: raw egg whites (avidin), TPN, antibiotics, genetic (biotinidase/holocarboxylase synthetase)
  • [ ] State biotin deficiency: scaly dermatitis (periorificial), alopecia, conjunctivitis, metabolic acidosis, organic aciduria
  • [ ] Explain pantothenic acid → CoA (acyl carrier) + ACP (fatty acid synthase); deficiency EXTREMELY RARE (burning feet syndrome, fatigue)

2. 2. Definitions / Key Concepts

TermDefinition
Pyridoxine (PN)Alcohol form; dietary; converted to PLP
Pyridoxal (PL)Aldehyde form; phosphorylated to PLP
Pyridoxamine (PM)Amine form; transamination intermediate
PLP (Pyridoxal 5'-Phosphate)Active cofactor; Schiff base with lysine ε-amino group; >140 enzymes
TransaminationPLP-dependent; amino acid + α-ketoacid ↔ new amino acid + new α-ketoacid; ALT, AST
DecarboxylationPLP-dependent: DOPA → dopamine (aromatic L-amino acid decarboxylase), 5-HTP → serotonin, glutamate → GABA (GAD), histidine → histamine
Glycogen PhosphorylasePLP-dependent; glycogen → glucose-1-P; muscle/liver; PLP = structural (not catalytic)
ALAS2 (δ-ALA Synthase 2)PLP-dependent; mitochondrial; glycine + succinyl-CoA → δ-ALA (haem synthesis); deficiency → sideroblastic anaemia
Sideroblastic AnaemiaRing sideroblasts (Fe-loaded mitochondria around nucleus); microcytic/hypochromic/dimorphic; ↑ferritin, ↑Fe, ↓TIBC
PLP Deficiency → ↓GABAGlutamate decarboxylase (GAD) requires PLP; ↓GABA → seizures (esp. infants), hyperirritability
Pyridoxine ToxicitySensory neuronopathy (dorsal column: loss vibration/position sense, ataxia); dose >100 mg/day chronic; reversible on cessation
INH/Penicillamine/Hydralazine/OCPForm hydrazones with PLP → ↑renal excretion; INH also inhibits pyridoxal kinase
Biotin (B7)Carboxylase cofactor: ATP + HCO₃⁻ + substrate → carboxylated substrate; holocarboxylase synthetase attaches to lysine
Acetyl-CoA Carboxylase (ACC)Acetyl-CoA → malonyl-CoA (rate-limiting fatty acid synthesis)
Pyruvate Carboxylase (PC)Pyruvate → oxaloacetate (anaplerosis, gluconeogenesis)
Propionyl-CoA Carboxylase (PCC)Propionyl-CoA → methylmalonyl-CoA (odd-chain FA, BCAA, cholesterol)
Methylcrotonyl-CoA Carboxylase (MCC)Leucine catabolism
AvidinRaw egg white glycoprotein; binds biotin 10¹⁵ affinity → biotin deficiency; heat denatures avidin
Biotinidase DeficiencyBTD mutation (AR) → cannot recycle biotin; late-onset (weeks–months); seizures, hypotonia, dermatitis, alopecia, organic aciduria
Holocarboxylase Synthetase DeficiencyHLCS mutation (AR) → cannot attach biotin to carboxylases; early-onset (neonatal); severe
Pantothenic Acid (B5)Component of CoA (4'-phosphopantetheine) + ACP (fatty acid synthase); ubiquitous in foods

3. 3. Core Content

1. Section 1: Pyridoxine (B6) Metabolism

Forms: Pyridoxine (PN, alcohol), pyridoxal (PL, aldehyde), pyridoxamine (PM, amine); interconvertible.

Absorption: Jejunum; passive diffusion; phosphorylated by pyridoxal kinase → PLP (liver, RBC, brain).

PLP-dependent enzymes (6 classes):

  1. Transaminases: ALT, AST, BCAT (BCAA) — amino group transfer
  2. Decarboxylases: DOPA decarboxylase, 5-HTP decarboxylase, GAD (glutamate → GABA), histidine decarboxylase
  3. Glycogen phosphorylase: Structural PLP; glycogenolysis
  4. Haem synthesis: ALAS2 (δ-ALA synthase 2) — erythroid-specific
  5. Cystathionine β-synthase (CBS): Homocysteine + serine → cystathionine (transsulfuration); PLP + B12 (methylcobalamin) for homocysteine remethylation
  6. Kynureninase: Tryptophan → niacin pathway; INH/B6 deficiency → inhibition

Catabolism: PLP → pyridoxic acid (excreted in urine); 4-pyridoxic acid = major metabolite.

2. Section 2: Pyridoxine Deficiency

Causes: INH, penicillamine, hydralazine, isoniazid, OCP, alcohol, malabsorption, dialysis, pregnancy, lactation, 5-FU, cycloserine, theophylline.

Clinical Features:

SystemFeatures
HaematologicalSideroblastic anaemia (↓ALAS2 → ↓haem → ring sideroblasts); microcytic/hypochromic/dimorphic; ↑serum Fe, ↑ferritin, ↓TIBC
NeurologicalPeripheral neuropathy (sensory, axonal); ↓GABA → seizures (infants), hyperirritability, EEG changes
DermatologicalCheilosis, angular stomatitis, glossitis, seborrhoeic dermatitis (nasolabial, scrotal)
Metabolic↓Cystathionine β-synthase → ↑homocysteine; ↓kynureninase → ↑xanthurenic acid (tryptophan load test)

Diagnosis: Plasma PLP <20 nmol/L (ref 20–150); erythrocyte aspartate aminotransferase activity coefficient (EAST-AC) >1.8; urinary 4-pyridoxic acid <2 µmol/day; tryptophan load test ↑xanthurenic acid.

3. Section 3: Pyridoxine Toxicity

Sensory neuronopathy: Dose >100 mg/day chronic (typically >500 mg/day); large sensory fibre degeneration (dorsal columns); loss of vibration/position sense, sensory ataxia; reversible on stopping (months); motor spared.

Mechanism: PLP competes with GABA?; direct toxicity to dorsal root ganglia.

Treatment: Stop pyridoxine; symptomatic; recovery over 6–24 months.

4. Section 4: Biotin (B7) Metabolism

Sources: Liver, egg yolk, nuts, seeds, salmon, avocado, sweet potato; gut flora synthesis (significant contribution).

Absorption: Small intestine; SMVT (sodium-dependent multivitamin transporter, SLC5A6); also colonic absorption from flora.

Holocarboxylase synthetase (HLCS): Attaches biotin to lysine ε-amino group of apocarboxylases (ATP-dependent).

Biotinidase (BTD): Recycles biotin from biocytin (biotin-lysine) and dietary protein-bound biotin; serum biotinidase activity = diagnostic.

Carboxylases (4 mitochondrial):

  1. Acetyl-CoA Carboxylase (ACC): Acetyl-CoA + HCO₃⁻ → malonyl-CoA (fatty acid synthesis rate-limiting)
  2. Pyruvate Carboxylase (PC): Pyruvate + HCO₃⁻ → oxaloacetate (anaplerosis, gluconeogenesis)
  3. Propionyl-CoA Carboxylase (PCC): Propionyl-CoA → methylmalonyl-CoA (odd-chain FA, BCAA: Val, Ile, Met, Thr; cholesterol side-chain)
  4. Methylcrotonyl-CoA Carboxylase (MCC): Leucine → β-methylcrotonyl-CoA → β-methylglutaconyl-CoA

5. Section 5: Biotin Deficiency

Causes:

  • Raw egg whites: Avidin binds biotin (Kd 10⁻¹⁵ M); heat denatures avidin
  • TPN without biotin (historical; now added)
  • Prolonged broad-spectrum antibiotics → ↓gut flora synthesis
  • Biotinidase deficiency (BTD): AR; profound (<10%) or partial (10–30%); neonatal screening (many countries)
  • Holocarboxylase synthetase deficiency (HLCS): AR; neonatal onset; severe
  • Anticonvulsants (valproate, carbamazepine, phenytoin, phenobarbital) → ↑biotin catabolism
  • Pregnancy/lactation → ↑demand, marginal status common

Clinical Features:

  • Dermatitis: Scaly, erythematous, periorificial (eyes, nose, mouth, perineum); "biotin-deficient facies"
  • Alopecia: Total (universalis) or patchy
  • Conjunctivitis: Blepharoconjunctivitis
