Amiodarone → type 1/2
Antithyroid drugs
Iodinated contrast
Goitrogens (cassava, cabbage)

9. 9. -Hour Recall Prompts

  1. RDA: 150 µg adult, 200 µg pregnancy/lactation
  2. Synthesis: I⁻ → NIS trap → Pendrin → TPO oxidation → iodination → MIT/DIT coupling → T3/T4
  3. I deficiency by age: foetus (cretinism) → newborn → child → adult
  4. Cretinism 2 types: neurological (MR, spastic) vs myxoedematous (dwarfism)
  5. USI: 20-40 mg I/kg salt (15-30 ppm); 88% global households
  6. Wolff-Chaikoff: I excess → hypothyroid; Jod-Basedow: I excess in I-deficit → hyper
  7. Amiodarone: 37% I, type 1 (I-induced) and type 2 (destructive)
  8. KI 130 mg for radiation protection (1-2h pre-exposure)

10. 10. -Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall Quality (1-5)Time SpentNotes
1
7
15
30

11. 11. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴RDA (200 µg pregnancy); synthesis pathway; deficiency by age; cretinism 2 types; USI 20–40 mg/kg; Wolff-Chaikoff vs Jod-Basedow; amiodarone 37% I; KI radiation protection; lithium hypothyroid; endemic goitre
Should Know 🟡NIS, Pendrin, TPO, thyroglobulin; UIC monitoring; T4→T3 conversion (deiodinase); goitrogens (cassava, cabbage); selenium cofactor; thyroglobulin population marker; lactating mothers
Nice to Know 🟢Perchlorate NIS inhibitor; I in Chernobyl aftermath; I fortification vehicles (bread, water); Iodine Global Network; Chernobyl thyroid cancer (I-131); seaweed toxicity (Japan)

12. 12. My Weak Points

  • [ ] Wolff-Chaikoff molecular mechanism detail
  • [ ] UIC field collection methodology
  • [ ] Pendrin syndrome genetics

13. 13. Self-Test Scorecard

DomainScore /10Target /10
Understanding8+
Recall8+
MCQ Performance8+
SBA Performance8+
Viva Confidence8+
TOTAL40+/50

14. 14. Exam Answer Modes

1. Long Answer / Essay (20 min)

Topic: "Iodine deficiency: spectrum, clinical features, and management"

  • Sources, RDA (200 µg pregnancy), absorption (NIS)
  • Synthesis: I⁻ → NIS → Pendrin → TPO oxidation → iodination → coupling → T3/T4
  • I deficiency spectrum by age: foetus (cretinism, stillbirth), newborn (neonatal hypothyroid), child (goitre, growth, cognitive), adult (goitre, hypothyroid)
  • Cretinism 2 types: neurological (MR, spastic, deaf, squint) vs myxoedematous (dwarfism, hypothyroid)
  • WHO USI programme: 20–40 mg I/kg salt; 88% global households
  • Treatment: iodised salt, lipiodol (remote), levothyroxine replacement
  • Excess: Wolff-Chaikoff (I excess → hypo), Jod-Basedow (I excess in I-deficient → hyper)
  • Drugs: amiodarone (37% I, type 1/2), lithium hypothyroid
  • Radiation: KI 130 mg within 1–2h

2. Short Note (7 min)

Topic: "Iodine Deficiency in Pregnancy"

  • Maternal I requirement: 200 µg/day (vs 150 µg adult); first-trimester critical for foetal brain development
  • Foetal I deficiency consequences: Cretinism (neurological, myxoedematous), congenital hypothyroidism, stillbirth, spontaneous abortion, pre-eclampsia
  • Prenatal supplementation: 150 µg iodine + dietary (dairy, eggs, seafood); ensure prenatal vitamin contains iodine
  • Hypothyroid in pregnancy: Levothyroxine (replacement; not just I); TSH target <2.5 mU/L (1st trimester), <3.0 (2nd/3rd)

3. Viva Answer (3 min)

Q: "Differentiate Wolff-Chaikoff from Jod-Basedow."

"A: Wolff-Chaikoff: Excess I⁻ in normal thyroid → transient organification block (TPO inhibition) → hypothyroidism; escape within 24–48h in normal; sustained in autoimmune (Hashimoto). Jod-Basedow: Excess I⁻ in I-deficient subject with autonomous nodules (multinodular goitre, Graves) → hyperthyroidism; treat with β-blocker, antithyroid if severe."

4. Ward Case Discussion (5 min)

Case: 25F pregnancy 8 weeks, no prenatal vitamins, mountainous region, hypothyroid symptoms. TSH 8, FT4 8 (low), anti-TPO+ (Hashimoto).

"Levothyroxine 50–100 µg/day (TSH target <2.5 mU/L first trimester); prenatal vitamin with 150 µg iodine + dietary; monitor TFT q4w first trimester, q8w thereafter; address mountain-region I deficiency (sea food, iodised salt). Counsel: foetal I critical first trimester; breastfeeding also requires I supplementation."

5. Last-Night-Before-Exam Sheet (1 min)

  • RDA: 150 µg adult, 200 µg pregnancy
  • Synthesis: I⁻ → NIS → Pendrin → TPO oxidation → iodination (MIT/DIT) → coupling → T3/T4
  • Deficiency by age: F-N-C-A = Foetus (cretinism), Newborn (neonatal hypo), Child (goitre/growth), Adult (goitre/hypothyroid)
  • Cretinism: Neurological (MR, spastic, deaf, squint) vs Myxoedematous (dwarfism, hypothyroid)
  • USI: 20–40 mg I/kg salt; 88% global households
  • Wolff-Chaikoff: I excess → hypo (organification block); Jod-Basedow: I excess in I-deficit → hyper
  • Amiodarone: 37% I; Type 1 (I-induced) + Type 2 (destructive)
  • KI radiation: 130 mg adults, within 1-2h of exposure
  • Lithium: Most common drug-induced hypothyroid
  • Pregnancy I: First trimester critical for foetal brain

15. 15. MCQs (10)

  1. RDA iodine for adult (non-pregnant):

A. 50 µg/day

B. 150 µg/day

C. 200 µg/day

D. 500 µg/day

E. 1000 µg/day

  1. RDA iodine for pregnancy:

A. 100 µg/day

B. 150 µg/day

C. 200 µg/day

D. 300 µg/day

E. 500 µg/day

  1. Thyroid peroxidase function:

A. NIS-mediated I⁻ trapping

B. I⁻ oxidation and iodination of tyrosine residues

C. Pendrin apical I⁻ efflux

D. T3/T4 release by endocytosis

E. Peripheral T4 → T3 conversion

  1. Universal Salt Iodisation (WHO) target iodine:

A. 5 mg/kg salt

B. 20–40 mg/kg salt

C. 100 mg/kg salt

D. 200 mg/kg salt

E. 500 mg/kg salt

  1. Neurological cretinism features include:

A. Severe growth retardation, dwarfism

B. Spastic quadriplegia, deaf-mutism, mental retardation

C. Severe hypothyroidism, myxoedema

D. Delayed bone age, dwarfism

E. Persistent jaundice

  1. Myxoedematous cretinism features include:

A. Spastic diplegia, deaf-mutism

B. Severe growth retardation, dwarfism, hypothyroidism

C. Mental retardation, squint

D. Gait disorder, spasticity

E. Deaf-mutism

  1. Wolff-Chaikoff effect describes:

A. Iodine excess → hyperthyroidism

B. Iodine excess → transient organification block (hypothyroidism)

C. Iodine excess → TSH elevation

D. Iodine excess → thyroid autonomy

E. Iodine excess → Graves' disease

  1. Jod-Basedow phenomenon describes:

A. I excess in normal → hypothyroid

B. I excess in I-deficient with autonomy → hyperthyroidism

C. I deficiency → goitre

D. I deficiency → cretinism

E. I excess → thyroiditis

  1. Amiodarone iodine content:

A. 5%

B. 10%

C. 37%

D. 70%

E. 90%

  1. Potassium iodide for radiation protection (adult dose):

A. 10 mg

B. 30 mg

C. 130 mg

D. 500 mg

E. 1 g

16. 16. SBA Questions (5)

  1. A 30-year-old woman is 8 weeks pregnant and lives in a mountainous area. She is not on prenatal vitamins. Which iodine dose is recommended?

A. 100 µg/day

B. 150 µg/day

C. 200 µg/day

D. 300 µg/day

E. 500 µg/day

  1. A 50-year-old man on amiodarone for atrial fibrillation develops weight loss, tremor, and tachycardia. TFT: TSH 0.05, FT4 35. Type 1 amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis. Best treatment?

A. Levothyroxine

B. Antithyroid drugs (PTU or MMI) + consider stopping amiodarone

C. Prednisolone

D. Radioactive iodine

E. Surgery

  1. A 40-year-old man on lithium for bipolar disorder has TSH 12, FT4 8. He is euthyroid clinically. Management?

A. Stop lithium

B. Continue lithium; start levothyroxine replacement

C. Switch to valproate

D. Add iodine

E. Wait and recheck in 6 months

  1. A 5-year-old child in a remote Himalayan village presents with severe growth retardation, deaf-mutism, and spastic diplegia. The region is endemic for iodine deficiency. Diagnosis?

A. Down syndrome

B. Neurological cretinism

C. Myxoedematous cretinism

D. Cerebral palsy

E. Rickets

  1. A 35-year-old man in a previously iodine-deficient area is given iodine supplementation. He develops palpitations, weight loss, and atrial fibrillation. TSH 0.02, FT4 45. Most likely cause?

A. Wolff-Chaikoff effect

B. Jod-Basedow phenomenon (I-induced hyperthyroidism in I-deficient subject with autonomous nodule)

C. Hashitoxicosis

D. Subacute thyroiditis

E. Graves' disease new onset

17. 17. Flashcards

  • Q: RDA iodine

A: 150 µg adult, 200 µg pregnancy/lactation

  • Q: Synthesis

A: I⁻ → NIS (trap) → Pendrin (efflux) → TPO (oxidation/iodination) → MIT/DIT coupling → T3/T4

  • Q: I deficiency by age

A: F-N-C-A = Foetus (cretinism), Newborn (neonatal hypo), Child (goitre/growth), Adult (goitre/hypothyroid)

  • Q: Neurological cretinism

A: Mental retardation, spastic quadriplegia, deaf-mutism, squint, gait disorder

  • Q: Myxoedematous cretinism

A: Severe growth retardation, dwarfism, hypothyroidism, myxoedema

  • Q: USI

A: 20-40 mg I/kg salt; 88% global households (WHO target ≥90%)

  • Q: Wolff-Chaikoff

A: I excess → transient organification block → hypothyroid (escape 24-48h)

  • Q: Jod-Basedow

A: I excess in I-deficient subject with autonomy → hyperthyroid

  • Q: Amiodarone

A: 37% I content; Type 1 (I-induced) + Type 2 (destructive thyroiditis)

  • Q: KI radiation

A: 130 mg adult, within 1-2h of exposure (blocks thyroid I-131 uptake >95%)

  • Q: Lithium

A: Most common drug-induced hypothyroid (inhibits T3/T4 release)

  • Q: Pregnancy I critical window

A: First trimester (foetal brain development); 200 µg/day required

18. 18. Answer Key with Explanations

1. MCQs

  1. B — RDA iodine 150 µg/day for adults; 200 µg for pregnancy/lactation.
  2. C — Pregnancy RDA iodine 200 µg/day (first-trimester critical for foetal brain development).
  3. B — Thyroid peroxidase (TPO) oxidises I⁻ and iodinates tyrosine residues on thyroglobulin; also catalyses MIT/DIT coupling.
  4. B — WHO USI target: 20–40 mg iodine per kg salt (15–30 ppm tropical; 30–80 ppm temperate).
  5. B — Neurological cretinism: spastic quadriplegia/diplegia, deaf-mutism, mental retardation; partially I-responsive.
  6. B — Myxoedematous cretinism: severe growth retardation (dwarfism), hypothyroidism, myxoedema; responds to I + thyroxine.
  7. B — Wolff-Chaikoff: excess I⁻ → transient TPO/organification block → hypothyroidism; escape in normal thyroid.
  8. B — Jod-Basedow: excess I⁻ in I-deficient subject with autonomous nodule → hyperthyroidism.
  9. C — Amiodarone 37% iodine by weight → type 1 (I-induced hyperthyroid) or type 2 (destructive thyroiditis).
  10. C — KI 130 mg adult dose for radiation protection; most effective within 1–2 hours of exposure.

2. SBAs

  1. C — Pregnancy I requirement 200 µg/day (vs 150 adult); first-trimester critical for foetal brain; ensure prenatal vitamin contains I.
  2. B — Type 1 amiodarone thyrotoxicosis: I-induced (pre-existing autonomous thyroid); treat with antithyroid drugs (PTU/MMI); consider stopping amiodarone.
  3. B — Lithium-induced hypothyroidism: continue lithium (mood control), add levothyroxine replacement; monitor TFT every 3 months.
  4. B — Neurological cretinism: I-deficient area + spastic diplegia + deaf-mutism; partially I-responsive.
  5. B — Jod-Basedow: I supplementation in I-deficient subject with autonomous nodule → hyperthyroidism; treat symptomatically + antithyroid.

19. 19. Summary

Iodine is a Must Know 🔴 topic for FCPS/MRCP.

Key takeaway: Essential for T3/T4; RDA 150 µg adult, 200 µg pregnancy; synthesis (NIS trap → Pendrin → TPO oxidation → iodination → MIT/DIT coupling → T3/T4). Deficiency spectrum by age: foetus (cretinism neurological/myxoedematous, stillbirth) → newborn (neonatal hypothyroid) → child (goitre, growth, cognitive) → adult (goitre, hypothyroid). USI 20–40 mg/kg salt (88% global). Wolff-Chaikoff = I excess → hypothyroid; Jod-Basedow = I excess in I-deficient → hyper. Amiodarone 37% I (type 1/2). Lithium most common drug-induced hypothyroid. KI 130 mg for radiation (1–2h pre).

Exam focus: Cretinism 2 types, USI, Wolff-Chaikoff vs Jod-Basedow, amiodarone thyroid, KI radiation, pregnancy I, lithium hypothyroid, endemic goitre.

Clinical relevance: Universal salt iodisation; pregnancy screening; amiodarone monitoring; lithium monitoring; KI stockpiles.

Template version: 1.0 | Davidson 24e Ch 22 aligned | FCPS/MRCP oriented

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 5.4.04 Selenium Copper Fluoride Manganese

Related: [[Nutritional Factors in Disease MOC]], [[Davidson Chapter 22 - Nutritional Factors in Disease Hierarchy]], [[../00_Index/Medicine MOC|Medicine MOC]]

[!important]
Selenium = selenocysteine (21st AA) in 25 selenoproteins (glutathione peroxidases, thioredoxin reductases, deiodinases); deficiency = Keshan disease (cardiomyopathy), Kashin-Beck (osteoarthropathy); Copper = cytochrome c oxidase, SOD1, caeruloplasmin; Wilson's (ATP7B), Menkes (ATP7A); Fluoride = dental/osteoporosis; Manganese = SOD2, glycosyltransferases.

1. 1. Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Describe selenium: selenocysteine, selenoproteins (GPx1-4, TRxR, deiodinases), RDA 55 µg, sources (Brazil nuts, organ meats, cereals)
  • [ ] Recognise selenium deficiency: Keshan disease (cardiomyopathy in children), Kashin-Beck (endemic osteoarthropathy), reversible myxedematous cretinism, infertility
  • [ ] State selenium excess: selenosis (alopecia, nail brittleness, garlic breath, GI upset, peripheral neuropathy); UL 400 µg/day
  • [ ] Describe copper metabolism: absorption (DMT1, duodenum), transport (albumin), storage (MT), excretion (biliary)
  • [ ] Identify copper deficiency: Menkes (kinky hair, neurodegeneration, hypopigmentation), acquired (bariatric, Zn excess, malabsorption)
  • [ ] State copper excess: Wilson's disease (ATP7B), Indian childhood cirrhosis, ICC
  • [ ] Explain fluoride: dental enamel fluoridation, anti-cariogenic, bone formation, RDA 3–4 mg, sources (water, tea, fish); toxicity = fluorosis

2. 2. Definitions / Key Concepts

TermDefinition
Selenocysteine (Sec)21st amino acid; UGA codon; co-translational insertion; selenoprotein active site
Selenoprotein P (SELENOP)Plasma carrier (60% plasma Se); also antioxidant; deficiency → Se deficiency
Glutathione Peroxidases (GPx1-4)Se-dependent; reduce H₂O₂/lipid peroxides (GPx4 in sperm); deficiency → oxidative stress
Thioredoxin Reductases (TRxR1-3)Se-dependent; DNA synthesis, redox signalling
Iodothyronine Deiodinases (DIO1-3)Se-dependent; T4 → T3 (DIO1/2) or rT3 (DIO3); cofactor for thyroid hormone metabolism
Keshan DiseaseEndemic cardiomyopathy in children (China, Keshan); responsive to Se; Coxsackie/influenza synergy; congestive cardiomyopathy
Kashin-Beck DiseaseEndemic osteoarthropathy (Tibet, China, Russia); joint enlargement, dwarfism, dystrophy; Se + iodine deficiency
SelenosisSe excess; alopecia, nail brittleness, garlic breath (dimethyl selenide), GI upset, paresthesia, peripheral neuropathy
Copper (Cu)Trace; 100 mg body; ceruloplasmin (95% plasma Cu), albumin, transcuprein
Cu/Zn-SOD (SOD1)Cytosolic superoxide dismutase; Cu + Zn; antioxidant
Cytochrome c Oxidase (CCO)Mitochondrial complex IV; Cu + heme a; ATP synthesis
Caeruloplasmin (Cp)95% plasma Cu; ferroxidase (Fe²⁺ → Fe³⁺); acute phase reactant
Menkes Disease (MD)X-linked recessive; ATP7A mutation; ↓Cu absorption, ↓Cu transport to brain; kinky hair, neurodegeneration, hypopigmentation; death by 3y
ATP7ACu-ATPase; intestinal Cu absorption, blood-brain barrier, placental
ATP7BCu-ATPase; hepatocyte Cu biliary excretion; Wilson's disease
Wilson's Disease (WD)AR; ATP7B mutation; ↓Cu biliary excretion, ↓ceruloplasmin binding; liver, brain (basal ganglia), eye (Kayser-Fleischer), kidney
Kayser-Fleischer RingCorneal Cu deposition in Descemet's membrane; WD; slit-lamp exam
FluorosisChronic F excess; dental mottling (mild), skeletal fluorosis (severe; osteosclerosis, calcification)
Manganese (Mn)Trace; cofactor (SOD2, arginase, pyruvate carboxylase, glycosyltransferases)
ManganismMn neurotoxicity (welders, miners); Parkinson-like (basal ganglia Mn deposition); chelation (EDTA, PAS)

3. 3. Core Content

1. Section 1: Selenium Metabolism

Forms: Selenocysteine (Sec, active in selenoproteins); selenomethionine (storage, non-specific incorporation); selenate, selenite (inorganic, supplements).

Absorption: Duodenum, jejunum; passive diffusion; selenomethionine absorbed like methionine.

Transport: Albumin; SELENOP (60% plasma Se); taken up by liver, brain, testes, kidney.

Excretion: Urine (trimethylselenonium); exhalation (dimethyl selenide, garlic odour); sweat.

RDA: 55 µg/day; UL 400 µg/day; intake varies by soil (China, USA high; Europe low).

Selenoproteins (25 in humans; key examples):

  • GPx1-4: Cytosolic (GPx1), GI (GPx2), plasma (GPx3), phospholipid hydroperoxide (GPx4, sperm)
  • Thioredoxin reductases (TRxR1-3): Redox regulation
  • Iodothyronine deiodinases (DIO1-3): T4 activation/inactivation
  • Selenoprotein P (SELENOP): Plasma transport, antioxidant
  • Selenoprotein K, S, W: Various functions

Functions: Antioxidant (GPx/TRxR); thyroid hormone metabolism (DIO); spermatogenesis (GPx4 in sperm); immunity; reproduction; anti-cancer (↓cancer risk in some).

2. Section 2: Selenium Deficiency & Diseases

Keshan Disease:

  • Geography: China (Keshan county, Heilongjiang), Siberia, Africa; Se-deficient soil
  • Age: Children 2–10y, women of childbearing age
  • Pathophysiology: Coxsackievirus B3/influenza A mutation in Se deficiency → virulent; cardiac antioxidant failure
  • Clinical: Congestive cardiomyopathy (cardiomegaly, arrhythmias, heart failure); often fatal
  • Prevention: Se supplementation (sodium selenite 0.5–1 mg/week) dramatically reduces incidence
  • Treatment: Selenium + cardiac support

Kashin-Beck Disease:

  • Geography: Tibet, China, Siberia, North Korea
  • Age: Children 5–15y
  • Pathophysiology: Se + iodine deficiency; possibly Fusarium mycotoxins
  • Clinical: Symmetrical osteoarthropathy (joint swelling, pain, loss of motion), short stature (dwarfism), dystrophic changes
  • Prevention: Se + iodine supplementation

Other Se Deficiency:

  • Myxedematous (endemic) cretinism: Se + iodine deficiency; responsive to Se + thyroxine
  • Cardiomyopathy (Western, non-endemic): Se deficiency post-TPN, HIV, dialysis
  • Sperm motility: Se supplementation may improve male fertility
  • Cancer: Observational: ↓Se = ↑cancer risk; SELECT trial: Se ± Vit E no prostate cancer benefit
  • Hashimoto's: ↓Se activity may worsen; supplementation may ↓TPO Ab (controversial)

3. Section 3: Selenium Toxicity (Selenosis)

Threshold: UL 400 µg/day (acute: 5 mg/kg)

Sources: Brazil nuts (high; up to 1917 µg/nut), supplements, occupational (metal refining)

Features: Alopecia, nail brittleness/transverse lines, garlic breath (dimethyl selenide), GI upset, peripheral neuropathy, fatigue, dermatitis, garlic sweat, myopathy.

4. Section 4: Copper Metabolism

Body content: 100–150 mg; liver, brain, heart, kidney.

Absorption: Duodenum, proximal jejunum; DMT1 (Fe²⁺/Cu²⁺ transporter); enterocyte basolateral exporter ATP7A; Cu bound to albumin → portal blood.

Hepatic processing: Cu → albumin → ceruloplasmin (Cp) → tissue; ATP7B exports Cu into bile for excretion; excess → stored as MT.

Excretion: Biliary (80%, faecal) = main route; urinary (<5%); sweat, menstrual blood, desquamation.

RDA: 0.9 mg/day (adult); UL 10 mg/day.

5. Section 5: Copper Deficiency

Causes:

CauseMechanism
Menkes diseaseATP7A mutation (XLR); ↓Cu absorption, ↓BBB transport, ↓placental
Acquired malabsorptionBariatric (RYGB), IBD, short bowel, CF, celiac
Excess ZnZn induces MT → Cu trapped in enterocyte → ↓Cu absorption (similar to WD Rx mechanism)
Excess ironCompetes with Cu absorption (DMT1)
TPN without CuMonths → deficiency
Penicillamine, trientineChelation (WD Rx)
Premature infants↓Hepatic stores, ↑demand

Menkes Disease (MD):

  • Genetics: XLR; ATP7A; 1:100,000; males affected
  • Pathophysiology: ↓Cu absorption, ↓Cu transport to CNS (failing BBB), ↓lysyl oxidase activity (kinky hair), ↓mitochondrial CCO, ↓SOD1
  • Clinical:
  • Hair: Sparse, brittle, kinky, hypopigmented, "steely" (pili torti on microscopy)
  • Skin: Hypopigmentation, lax, "cutis laxa" (↓elastin cross-linking)
  • CNS: Progressive neurodegeneration, seizures, hypotonia, MR; death by 3 years (without treatment)
  • Other: Hypothermia, bone abnormalities (metaphyseal dysplasia, osteoporosis), vascular tortuosity, bladder diverticula
  • Diagnosis: ↓Cu, ↓Cp, abnormal Cu uptake in fibroblasts; mutation ATP7A
  • Treatment: Subcutaneous Cu-histidine (early; before 28d; experimental) — modest neurological benefit; Cu chloride IV also used; paroxetine (controversial)

Acquired Cu deficiency:

  • Post-bariatric (RYGB): Cu deficiency in 10–20% within 2 years
  • Hyperzincuria (excess Zn): Wilson's Rx, denture cream
  • B12 / Fe / Cu supplementation: B12 + Fe may not be enough; add Cu
  • TPN: Add Cu 0.5–1.5 mg/day
  • Clinical: Myeloneuropathy (similar to B12 — dorsal column), ataxia, peripheral neuropathy, anaemia (Fe-resistant), neutropenia, hyperpigmentation (skin, hair)
  • Differential: B12 deficiency, Zn excess, severe malabsorption

6. Section 6: Wilson's Disease (See also Zinc file)

  • AR; ATP7B mutation (chromosome 13); ↓Cu biliary excretion, ↓Cp binding, ↑Cu accumulation
  • Hepatic: Steatosis → hepatitis → cirrhosis → HCC; acute liver failure
  • Neurological: Basal ganglia (putamen, globus pallidus, caudate); movement disorders (tremor, dystonia, parkinsonism, choreoathetosis, dysarthria)
  • Psychiatric: Depression, psychosis, personality change
  • Ocular: Kayser-Fleischer ring (Descemet's membrane Cu); sunflower cataract
  • Renal: Fanconi syndrome, nephrolithiasis
  • Haematological: Haemolytic anaemia (Coombs-negative; free Cu in serum)
  • Treatment: Penicillamine (chelator; first-line for acute neurological), Trientine (alternative chelator), Zinc (induces MT; maintenance/pregnancy/children), Liver transplantation (end-stage)

7. Section 7: Copper Excess

Wilson's disease: See above

Indian Childhood Cirrhosis (ICC): Historically high mortality; infant Cu exposure (vessels, milk); sporadic ICC in some populations

Acute Cu poisoning: Self-harm (sulphate ingestion); GI bleeding, methaemoglobinaemia, haemolysis, hepatic necrosis, shock

Chronic Cu excess: Industrial exposure, contaminated water (Wilson disease carriers, haemochromatosis)

8. Section 8: Fluoride

Sources: Water (1 ppm optimal), tea (high), fish (with bones), toothpaste (NaF), salt (some countries), supplements

RDA: 3 mg (M) / 3 mg (F); UL 10 mg/day

Functions:

  • Dental: Enamel fluorapatite (resistant to acid); anti-cariogenic; ↓demineralisation, ↑remineralisation
  • Bone: Hydroxyfluoroapatite; ↓osteoclast activity, ↑osteoblast; ↓fracture in optimal range
  • Topical: Toothpaste, mouthwash, varnish

Deficiency: Rare (not essential); ↑dental caries, possibly osteoporosis

Excess (Fluorosis):

  • Dental (mild, <4 ppm F): Mottling, white spots, brown staining; cosmetic
  • Skeletal (chronic, >4 ppm F): Osteosclerosis (↑trabecular bone), calcification of ligaments, osteophytes, bone pain, ↑fracture risk
  • Severe (industrial): Crippling fluorosis; vertebrae fusion, nerve compression, disability
  • Acute toxicity: GI upset, salivation, hyperreflexia, seizures (rare; very high dose)

9. Section 9: Manganese

Sources: Whole grains, nuts, legumes, tea; absorption 1–5%

RDA: 2.3 mg (M) / 1.8 mg (F); UL 11 mg/day

Functions: SOD2 (mitochondrial; Mn); pyruvate carboxylase; arginase; glutamine synthetase; glycosyltransferases (proteoglycan synthesis)

Deficiency (rare): Bone demineralisation, hair depigmentation, dermatitis, weight loss, hypercholesterolaemia (animals)

Toxicity (Manganism):

  • Sources: Welders (Mn fumes), miners, contaminated water
  • Pathology: Mn deposition in basal ganglia (especially globus pallidus)
  • Clinical: Parkinson-like — bradykinesia, rigidity, postural instability, dystonia, gait disturbance, neuropsychiatric
  • Diagnosis: T1-weighted MRI hyperintensity in basal ganglia
  • Treatment: Stop exposure; chelation (EDTA, PAS, levodopa, trientine); antioxidants
  • Differentiation: Responds poorly to levodopa; more dystonia; ↑Mn on imaging

4. 4. Clinical Correlation

ScenarioActionNotes
5y child, Keshan county China, heart failure, ↓SeSodium selenite 0.5–1 mg/week; cardiac supportKeshan disease; Se dramatically improves
12y Tibetan child, joint pain, swollen fingers, dwarfismSe + iodine supplementation; physiotherapyKashin-Beck; endemic area
60M, RYGB 3y ago, ataxia, spastic gait, dorsiflexion loss, normal B12Serum Cu, Cp, 24h urinary Cu; IV Cu (if severe)Cu deficiency myeloneuropathy
30F, Wilson's disease, neurological, started on penicillamine, progressiveAdd Zn 50 mg ×3 (empty stomach); consider trientine switch; diet (avoid Cu-rich foods)WD therapy
5m boy, kinky hair, seizures, hypotonia, ↓Cu, ↓CpATP7A testing → Menkes; Cu-histidine SC (early); paroxetine; supportiveMenkes; fatal by 3y untreated
65F, dental mottling, well-water area (F 8 ppm)Avoid water source; dental cosmetic Rx; skeletal assessment if symptomaticFluorosis (mild)
45M, welder, Parkinson-like, MRI T1 basal gangliaStop Mn exposure; chelation (EDTA, PAS); trientineManganism; levodopa-resistant
50F, RYGB 2y, anaemia not responding to Fe and B12, neutropenia, hyperpigmentationCheck Cu, Cp; if low, IV Cu + oral Cu gluconate 2–4 mg/dayCu deficiency in bariatric

5. 5. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- Must know: Se = selenocysteine, 25 selenoproteins (GPx, TRxR, DIO); Keshan (cardiomyopathy in children, China) + Kashin-Beck (osteoarthropathy, Tibet); RDA 55 µg; selenosis (garlic breath, alopecia); Cu = 100 mg, caeruloplasmin, ATP7A/ATP7B; Menkes (ATP7A, kinky hair, neurodegeneration, fatal <3y); Wilson's (ATP7B, KF ring, liver/brain); RDA 0.9 mg; F = dental fluorapatide, fluorosis (mottling, skeletal); Mn = SOD2, manganism (Parkinson-like)
- Common viva: Keshan disease, Kashin-Beck, selenosis garlic breath; Menkes disease (ATP7A, kinky hair); Wilson's disease (ATP7B); Cu deficiency in RYGB; fluoride fluorosis; manganism in welders
- Exam trap: Confusing Menkes (XLR) with Wilson's (AR); missing Cu deficiency in RYGB; confusing selenosis with arsenic toxicity; Mn toxicity levodopa-resistant

6. 6. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
Menkes = Wilson'sMenkes: XLR, ATP7A, kinky hair, fatal <3y; Wilson's: AR, ATP7B, liver/brain, treatable
Cu deficiency = bariatricALL bariatric patients need Cu screening (in addition to Fe, B12, Ca, D)
Selenosis = acute toxicityChronic selenosis from supplements/Brazil nuts; garlic breath (dimethyl selenide)
Keshan only in ChinaEndemic in Se-deficient areas globally; sporadic cases in malnourished
Manganism = Parkinson'sMn: T1 hyperintensity basal ganglia, dystonia prominent, levodopa-resistant
Fluorosis = dentalSkeletal fluorosis from chronic F excess (industrial, water >4 ppm)
Se supplementation always safeUL 400 µg; selenosis; SELECT trial: Se ± Vit E no cancer benefit
Wilson's: penicillamine always firstZn maintenance/pregnancy/paediatrics; CHELATE trial Zn non-inferior
Cu/Cp always low in Cu deficiencyCp is acute phase reactant (↑in inflammation); Cp may be normal in Cu deficiency with inflammation

7. 7. Mnemonics

  • Selenoproteins: GPx-TRxR-DIO = Glutathione Peroxidases, ThioRedoxin Reductases, DeIOdinases
  • Keshan: Keshan = Kardiomyopathy; China, Coxsackie, Children
  • Kashin-Beck: Kashin-Beck = Knees (osteoarthropathy); Tibet, Children 5-15y
  • Selenosis: Garlic breath, Alopecia, Nail brittleness = GAN
  • Menkes: Menkes = Mutation in ATP7A = Kinky Hair; XLR
  • Wilson's: Wilson's = ATP7B; Liver, Brain, Eye, Kidney, Anaemia = LB-EKA
  • Caeruloplasmin: 95% plasma Cu; CoP Cu; acute phase reactant
  • Fluoride: For dental Fluorapatite; Doses 1 ppm water
  • Manganism: Manganese = Motor (basal ganglia); welder's Parkinson
  • Cu absorption: DMT1 (with Fe); ATP7A (enterocyte export, menkes); ATP7B (hepatocyte biliary, Wilson's)

8. 8. Mind Map

Se, Cu, F, Mn
Selenium

– Keshan: cardiomyopathy, China
– Kashin-Beck: osteoarthropathy, Tibet
– Myxedematous cretinism
Excess: Selenosis
– Garlic breath, alopecia, nail
Copper
100-150 mg

ATP7B (Wilson's)

– Acquired: RYGB, Zn excess, TPN
– Myeloneuropathy, anaemia
Excess: Wilson's disease

Sources: water, tea, fish
Fluorosis: dental mottling, skeletal
Manganese

Manganism (welder's Parkinson)
– T1 basal ganglia
– Levodopa-resistant

9. 9. -Hour Recall Prompts

  1. Se: selenocysteine, 25 selenoproteins (GPx, TRxR, DIO); RDA 55 µg
  2. Keshan: cardiomyopathy in children (China, Coxsackie); Kashin-Beck: osteoarthropathy
  3. Selenosis: garlic breath, alopecia, nail brittleness
  4. Cu: caeruloplasmin (95%); ATP7A (Menkes), ATP7B (Wilson's)
  5. Menkes: XLR, kinky hair, neurodegeneration, fatal <3y
  6. Wilson's: AR, KF ring, liver/brain, treatable (penicillamine, Zn, trientine)
  7. Cu deficiency in RYGB: myeloneuropathy, anaemia
  8. Mn: SOD2; manganism in welders; levodopa-resistant

10. 10. -Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall Quality (1-5)Time SpentNotes
1
7
15
30

11. 11. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴Se selenoproteins (GPx, TRxR, DIO); Keshan cardiomyopathy; Kashin-Beck osteoarthropathy; selenosis (garlic breath, alopecia); Cu caeruloplasmin; Menkes (ATP7A, kinky hair, XLR); Wilson's (ATP7B, KF ring); Cu deficiency in RYGB; fluoride fluorosis; Mn manganism
Should Know 🟡SELECT trial (Se no prostate cancer benefit); myxedematous cretinism; Cu histidine SC in Menkes; trientine in WD; CHELATE trial; Mn T1 MRI basal ganglia; dental fluorosis; skeletal fluorosis; ICC
Nice to Know 🟢Se in Hashimoto's; Se in male fertility; Se in HIV/cancer; paroxetine in Menkes; acute Cu poisoning; Cu in Indian childhood cirrhosis; DMT1 in Cu/Fe absorption; MT in Cu homeostasis

12. 12. My Weak Points

  • [ ] Kashin-Beck pathophysiology detail
  • [ ] Menkes Cu-histidine dosing
  • [ ] Mn chelation specifics

13. 13. Self-Test Scorecard

DomainScore /10Target /10
Understanding8+
Recall8+
MCQ Performance8+
SBA Performance8+
Viva Confidence8+
TOTAL40+/50

14. 14. Exam Answer Modes

1. Long Answer / Essay (20 min)

Topic: "Selenium, Copper, Fluoride, Manganese: functions, deficiency, and toxicity"

  • Se: selenocysteine, 25 selenoproteins (GPx, TRxR, DIO); Keshan (cardiomyopathy in children, China), Kashin-Beck (osteoarthropathy, Tibet); selenosis (garlic breath, alopecia)
  • Cu: caeruloplasmin (95%), ATP7A/ATP7B; Menkes (XLR, kinky hair, fatal <3y); Wilson's (AR, liver/brain, KF ring); Cu deficiency in RYGB
  • Fluoride: dental fluorapatite, anti-cariogenic; fluorosis (mottling, skeletal)
  • Mn: SOD2; manganism in welders; T1 basal ganglia; levodopa-resistant

2. Short Note (7 min)

Topic: "Menkes Disease"

  • XLR; ATP7A mutation; ↓Cu absorption, ↓BBB transport, ↓lysyl oxidase (kinky hair), ↓mitochondrial CCO
  • Clinical: Sparse, brittle, kinky hair (pili torti); hypopigmentation; progressive neurodegeneration; seizures; hypotonia; bone abnormalities; death by 3 years
  • Diagnosis: ↓Cu, ↓Cp, abnormal Cu uptake in fibroblasts, ATP7A mutation
  • Treatment: Subcutaneous Cu-histidine (early, <28d, experimental); paroxetine (controversial); supportive

3. Viva Answer (3 min)

Q: "Differentiate Menkes from Wilson's disease."

"A: Menkes: XLR, ATP7A, ↓Cu absorption, kinky hair, neurodegeneration, fatal by 3y; no effective treatment. Wilson's: AR, ATP7B, ↓Cu biliary excretion, KF ring, liver cirrhosis + basal ganglia degeneration, treatable (penicillamine, trientine, Zn). Both: ↓Cu transport, but Menkes = systemic deficiency; Wilson's = accumulation."

4. Ward Case Discussion (5 min)

Case: 50F, RYGB 2y ago, progressive ataxia, dorsal column signs, Fe-resistant anaemia, neutropenia, hyperpigmentation, B12 and folate normal.

"Diagnosis: Acquired Cu deficiency post-bariatric. Check Cu, Cp, 24h urinary Cu; IV Cu chloride (severe); oral Cu gluconate 2–4 mg/day maintenance; monitor Hb, neutropenia. Add multivitamin with Cu, Fe, B12, D, Ca long-term."

5. Last-Night-Before-Exam Sheet (1 min)

  • Se: Selenocysteine; 25 selenoproteins (GPx, TRxR, DIO); Keshan (cardiomyopathy, China, children), Kashin-Beck (osteoarthropathy, Tibet); selenosis (garlic breath, alopecia, nail)
  • Cu: Caeruloplasmin (95% plasma Cu); ATP7A (Menkes), ATP7B (Wilson's)
  • Menkes: XLR, ATP7A, kinky hair (pili torti), neurodegeneration, fatal <3y
  • Wilson's: AR, ATP7B, liver/brain, KF ring, treatable (penicillamine, trientine, Zn)
  • Cu deficiency in RYGB: Myeloneuropathy, Fe-resistant anaemia, neutropenia
  • Fluorosis: Dental mottling (mild), skeletal (severe); F in water 1 ppm optimal
  • Mn: SOD2; manganism in welders (Parkinson-like, levodopa-resistant); T1 MRI basal ganglia
  • Wilson's Rx: Penicillamine, Trientine, Zinc (induces MT; maintenance)

15. 15. MCQs (10)

  1. Selenocysteine is the 21st amino acid; key selenoproteins include:

A. ALA synthase, ferrochelatase

B. Glutathione peroxidase, thioredoxin reductase, deiodinase

C. Cytochrome P450, ALAD

D. Lactase, sucrase

E. Hexokinase, glucokinase

  1. Keshan disease is endemic cardiomyopathy in:

A. Africa (malnutrition)

B. China (Keshan county, Heilongjiang)

C. India (vitamin deficiency)

D. South America (altitude)

E. Europe (industrial)

  1. Selenosis clinical features include:

A. Blue-grey discolouration

B. Garlic breath, alopecia, nail brittleness

C. Hypothyroidism only

D. Rash only

E. Foetor hepaticus

  1. Caeruloplasmin carries what % of plasma copper:

A. 5%

B. 30%

C. 50%

D. 95%

E. 100%

  1. Menkes disease mutation:

A. ATP7B

B. ATP7A

C. Cu/Zn-SOD

D. Hephaestin

E. Cytochrome c oxidase

  1. Menkes disease hallmark clinical feature:

A. KF ring

B. Kinky hair (pili torti) + neurodegeneration

C. Hypopigmented macules

D. Blue sclerae

E. Coombs-positive haemolysis

  1. Wilson's disease copper-ATPase:

A. ATP7A

B. ATP7B

C. SOD1

D. Albumin

E. Caeruloplasmin

  1. Copper deficiency in post-bariatric patients classically causes:

A. Megaloblastic anaemia

B. Myeloneuropathy (dorsal column) + Fe-resistant anaemia

C. Hyperparathyroidism

D. Adrenal insufficiency

E. Pulmonary fibrosis

  1. Manganism (welder's Parkinson) MRI finding:

A. Caudate atrophy

B. T1-weighted hyperintensity in basal ganglia (especially globus pallidus)

C. Substantia nigra hyperintensity

D. White matter lesions

E. Cerebellar atrophy

  1. Fluoride toxicity at chronic high water exposure (>4 ppm):

A. Dental mottling, skeletal fluorosis (osteosclerosis, calcification)

B. Acute liver failure

C. Hypothyroidism

D. Adrenal crisis

E. Bone marrow suppression

16. 16. SBA Questions (5)

  1. A 5-month-old boy with kinky hair, hypotonia, seizures, hypopigmentation, ↓Cu, ↓Cp. Most likely diagnosis?

A. Kwashiorkor

B. Menkes disease (ATP7A mutation)

C. Wilson's disease (very young)

D. Albinism

E. Ectodermal dysplasia

  1. A 25-year-old woman with Wilson's disease, neurological symptoms, started on penicillamine. Best adjunct therapy?

A. Vitamin C

B. Zinc 50 mg ×3/day (empty stomach) to induce metallothionein

C. Iron supplementation

D. Calcium

E. Vitamin E

  1. A 50-year-old welder presents with bradykinesia, rigidity, dystonia, postural instability. T1-weighted MRI shows hyperintensity in the globus pallidus. Best treatment?

A. Levodopa/carbidopa

B. Stop Mn exposure; consider chelation (EDTA, PAS)

C. Anticholinergics

D. Dopamine agonists

E. Steroids

  1. A 30-year-old man from Keshan county, China, presents with congestive cardiomyopathy in childhood. Most likely nutritional deficiency?

A. Vitamin B1

B. Selenium (Keshan disease)

C. Iodine

D. Vitamin D

E. Iron

  1. A 60-year-old woman post-RYGB presents with progressive ataxia, dorsal column signs, Fe-resistant anaemia, neutropenia. B12 normal. Most likely cause?

A. Vitamin B12 deficiency

B. Copper deficiency (post-bariatric)

C. Folate deficiency

D. Vitamin E deficiency

E. Niacin deficiency

17. 17. Flashcards

  • Q: Selenoproteins

A: GPx, TRxR, DIO (deiodinases)

  • Q: Keshan disease

A: Endemic cardiomyopathy in children (China); Se + Coxsackie synergy

  • Q: Kashin-Beck disease

A: Endemic osteoarthropathy (Tibet); Se + I deficiency

  • Q: Selenosis features

A: Garlic breath (dimethyl selenide), alopecia, nail brittleness, paresthesia

  • Q: Caeruloplasmin %

A: 95% of plasma Cu

  • Q: Menkes gene

A: ATP7A (XLR); ↓Cu absorption, BBB

  • Q: Menkes features

A: Kinky hair (pili torti), neurodegeneration, hypopigmentation, fatal <3y

  • Q: Wilson's gene

A: ATP7B (AR); ↓Cu biliary excretion

  • Q: Wilson's features

A: KF ring, basal ganglia (movement), liver, kidney, Coombs-ve HA

  • Q: Cu deficiency post-bariatric

A: Myeloneuropathy + Fe-resistant anaemia + neutropenia

  • Q: Fluoride functions

A: Dental fluorapatite, anti-cariogenic, bone formation

  • Q: Fluorosis (chronic F excess)

A: Dental mottling (mild), skeletal osteosclerosis + calcification (severe)

  • Q: Manganism

A: Welder's Parkinson; T1 basal ganglia; levodopa-resistant

  • Q: Mn function

A: SOD2 (mitochondrial); pyruvate carboxylase; arginase

18. 18. Answer Key with Explanations

1. MCQs

  1. B — Key selenoproteins: glutathione peroxidases (GPx1-4, antioxidant), thioredoxin reductases (TRxR1-3, redox), iodothyronine deiodinases (DIO1-3, T4→T3).
  2. B — Keshan disease: endemic cardiomyopathy in children in Keshan county (Heilongjiang, China) and other Se-deficient soil areas; Coxsackie/influenza synergy.
  3. B — Selenosis: garlic breath (dimethyl selenide), alopecia, nail brittleness, GI upset, paresthesia, peripheral neuropathy.
  4. D — Caeruloplasmin (Cp) carries 95% of plasma Cu; also ferroxidase (Fe²⁺→Fe³⁺) and acute phase reactant.
  5. B — Menkes disease: ATP7A mutation; X-linked recessive; ↓Cu absorption and ↓BBB Cu transport.
  6. B — Menkes hallmark: kinky/brittle hair (pili torti on microscopy) + neurodegeneration; fatal by 3y if untreated.
  7. B — Wilson's disease: ATP7B mutation; ↓Cu biliary excretion, ↓Cp binding; Cu accumulation in liver, brain, eye (KF ring).
  8. B — Post-bariatric Cu deficiency: myeloneuropathy (dorsal column signs, similar to B12), Fe-resistant anaemia, neutropenia, hyperpigmentation.
  9. B — Manganism MRI: T1-weighted hyperintensity in basal ganglia (especially globus pallidus); differentiates from Parkinson's (no specific MRI).
  10. A — Fluorosis: chronic F excess (water >4 ppm); dental mottling (mild, cosmetic), skeletal fluorosis (severe; osteosclerosis, calcification of ligaments, joint restriction).

2. SBAs

  1. B — Menkes disease: 5-month boy, kinky hair (pili torti), hypotonia, seizures, hypopigmentation, ↓Cu, ↓Cp; XLR ATP7A mutation; fatal by 3y.
  2. B — Wilson's neurological: penicillamine + Zn (induces metallothionein, blocks Cu absorption; adjuvant/maintenance).
  3. B — Manganism: welder + Parkinson-like + T1 basal ganglia hyperintensity; stop Mn exposure, consider chelation (EDTA, PAS); levodopa-resistant.
  4. B — Keshan disease: Se deficiency + Coxsackie synergy → congestive cardiomyopathy in children (Keshan county, China).
  5. B — Post-bariatric Cu deficiency: myeloneuropathy (dorsal column), Fe-resistant anaemia, neutropenia; B12 normal distinguishes from B12 deficiency.

19. 19. Summary

Selenium, Copper, Fluoride, Manganese are Must Know 🔴 topics for FCPS/MRCP.

Key takeaway: Se = selenocysteine, 25 selenoproteins (GPx, TRxR, DIO); Keshan (cardiomyopathy in children, China), Kashin-Beck (osteoarthropathy, Tibet); selenosis (garlic breath, alopecia). Cu = caeruloplasmin (95%); Menkes (ATP7A, XLR, kinky hair, fatal <3y); Wilson's (ATP7B, AR, liver/brain, KF ring); Cu deficiency in RYGB (myeloneuropathy, Fe-resistant anaemia). Fluoride = dental fluorapatite, anti-cariogenic, fluorosis (mottling, skeletal). Mn = SOD2; manganism in welders (Parkinson-like, levodopa-resistant).

Exam focus: Se selenoproteins, Keshan/Kashin-Beck, selenosis, caeruloplasmin, Menkes vs Wilson's, Cu deficiency, fluoride, manganism.

Clinical relevance: RYGB Cu screening; Wilson's monitoring; Se in endemic areas; Mn occupational protection; fluoridation.

Template version: 1.0 | Davidson 24e Ch 22 aligned | FCPS/MRCP oriented

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 5.4.05 Zinc Acrodermatitis Wound Healing

Related: [[Nutritional Factors in Disease MOC]], [[Davidson Chapter 22 - Nutritional Factors in Disease Hierarchy]], [[../00_Index/Medicine MOC|Medicine MOC]]

[!important]
Zinc = 2nd most abundant trace element; 300+ zinc finger proteins, 1000+ Zn-dependent transcription factors; structural (Zn finger motif), catalytic (Zn metalloenzymes), regulatory; RDA 8 M/11 F mg; deficiency = acrodermatitis enteropathica, alopecia, immune dysfunction, poor wound healing, taste/smell loss; Wilson's disease (Zn competes with Cu).

1. 1. Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Describe zinc absorption (ZIP4, enterocyte), distribution (50% muscle, 30% bone), plasma binding (albumin 60%, α2-macroglobulin 30%), excretion (pancreatic/intestinal)
  • [ ] Explain 3 functions: structural (Zn fingers, Zn twists), catalytic (Zn metalloenzymes), regulatory (transcription factors, immune)
  • [ ] Recognise acrodermatitis enteropathica: periorificial dermatitis, alopecia, diarrhoea, irritability; SLC39A4 (ZIP4) mutation; treatment Zn 3 mg/kg/day
  • [ ] Identify at-risk: malabsorption (Crohn's, coeliac, RYGB, alcohol), bariatric, chronic liver disease, dialysis, premature infants, vegan, pregnancy, burns
  • [ ] State acquired Zn deficiency: dermatitis (perioral, perianal), alopecia, taste/smell loss, immune (T-cell ↓), wound healing, fertility
  • [ ] Explain zinc in Wilson's disease: Zn induces metallothionein in enterocyte → binds Cu → ↓Cu absorption; adults 50 mg ×3/day, children 25 mg ×3/day
  • [ ] Recognise Zn excess (acute): nausea, vomiting, GI distress, headache; chronic: Cu deficiency, ↓immune

2. 2. Definitions / Key Concepts

TermDefinition
Zinc (Zn²⁺)Essential trace element; 2–3 g body (60% muscle, 30% bone, 0.1% plasma); structural/catalytic/regulatory
ZIP4 (SLC39A4)Apical enterocyte Zn importer; gene mutation = acrodermatitis enteropathica (AR)
ZnT1 (SLC30A1)Basolateral Zn exporter (enterocyte)
ZIP10, ZIP11, ZnT2, ZnT3Tissue-specific Zn transporters; ZIP = import, ZnT = transport out of cytosol
ZnT2 (SLC30A2)Mammary gland Zn transport; mutation = transient neonatal Zn deficiency (low breast milk Zn)
Metallothionein (MT)Cysteine-rich Zn/Cu-binding protein; Zn induces MT in enterocyte → binds Cu → ↓Cu absorption; antioxidant
Zn Finger MotifCys2His2 (most common) or Cys4 structure; Zn tetrahedrally coordinates; DNA/RNA/protein binding
Zn Superoxide Dismutase (SOD1)Zn/Cu-dependent; antioxidant; Cu-Zn SOD
Zn Metalloenzymes>300; alcohol dehydrogenase, carbonic anhydrase, alkaline phosphatase, carboxypeptidase, DNA/RNA polymerase
MTF1Metal-responsive transcription factor; Zn sensing → MT gene expression
Acrodermatitis Enteropathica (AE)AR; SLC39A4 (ZIP4) mutation; dermatitis (perioral/perianal), alopecia, diarrhoea, irritability
Periorificial DermatitisErythematous, scaly, vesiculobullous; perioral, perianal, perineal, acral; Zn deficiency
Dysseborrhoeic DermatitisZn deficiency rash in seborrheic distribution; differentiates from vitamin B3/B6 deficiency
Beau's LinesTransverse nail ridges; Zn, severe illness, chemo
Hypogonadism (Zn)↓Testosterone, ↓spermatogenesis, delayed puberty; Zn-dependent testosterone synthesis
Wilson's DiseaseATP7B mutation → Cu overload; Zn therapy (induces MT)
Acute Zn DeficiencyNUTRI-IV (Nutritional Intervention), TPN without Zn, penicillamine
Wilson + Zn TrialZn vs penicillamine (CHELATE trial; Zn non-inferior for neurological WD)
Sideroblastic Anaemia (Cu Def)Cu deficiency (Zn excess) → ↓hephaestin → ↓Fe utilisation → ring sideroblasts

3. 3. Core Content

1. Section 1: Zinc Metabolism

Body content: 2–3 g in adult; 60% muscle, 30% bone, 0.1% plasma; liver, kidney, brain, retina also rich.

Absorption: Duodenum, proximal jejunum; ZIP4 (SLC39A4) apical import; passive diffusion at high dose; ZnT1 basolateral export; MT sequesters in enterocyte.

Regulation: ZIP4 expression ↑in Zn deficiency (adaptive); MT binds Zn (prevents overshoot); paracellular (tight junctions) at high dose.

Enhancers: Animal protein (muscle, fish, eggs), histidine, cysteine.

Inhibitors: Phytates (whole grains, legumes), oxalates, polyphenols, Fe (competes), Cu (competes), Ca (dairy), oral contraceptives.

Plasma: Bound to albumin 60%, α2-macroglobulin 30%, transferrin 10%; 0.1% free; plasma Zn poorly reflects tissue stores.

Excretion: Pancreatic (Zn in pancreatic juice) 30%; intestinal (desquamation); sweat (minor); renal 10% (filtered + reabsorbed).

Tissue turnover: Slow; body pool replaced every 1–2 weeks.

2. Section 2: Functions

  1. Structural: >300 zinc finger proteins (DNA/RNA binding, transcription factors); steroid receptors (oestrogen, androgen, glucocorticoid); receptor tyrosine kinases; GPCRs; cytoskeletal (tubulin).
  2. Catalytic: >300 Zn metalloenzymes:
  • Alcohol dehydrogenase (Zn + NAD⁺)
  • Carbonic anhydrase (CO₂ + H₂O ↔ H₂CO₃; Zn + His)
  • Alkaline phosphatase (Zn + Mg²⁺)
  • Carboxypeptidases (pancreatic)
  • RNA polymerase (DNA transcription)
  • Reverse transcriptase (HIV)
  • Matrix metalloproteinases (collagen degradation)
  • Cu/Zn-SOD (SOD1, antioxidant)
  1. Regulatory: Transcription factors, immune (T-cell development, cytokine production), signal transduction, wound healing (collagen synthesis, epithelial cell migration), growth/development, fertility/testosterone, antioxidant, apoptosis.

3. Section 3: Requirements & Sources

RDA (IOM 2001):

  • Adult male: 11 mg/day (was 15 mg)
  • Adult female: 8 mg/day
  • Pregnancy: 11 mg/day (UK 7; some 12–15)
  • Lactation: 12 mg/day
  • Children: 2–8 mg by age
  • Smokers/alcohol: +20–30%

UL: 40 mg/day (acute toxicity); chronic high → Cu deficiency

Sources (mg/100 g):

  • Oysters 78, Beef 12, Pumpkin seeds 10, Cashews 5, Chickpeas 2, Lentils 1.5
  • Animal protein > plant (phytate binding reduces bioavailability)

4. Section 4: Acrodermatitis Enteropathica (AE)

Genetics: AR; SLC39A4 (ZIP4) mutation on chromosome 8q24; loss of ZIP4 function → ↓Zn absorption; presents after weaning (breast milk Zn ligand compensates partially).

Classic Triad:

  1. Dermatitis: Perioral, perianal, perineal, acral, hands, feet; erythematous, scaly, vesiculobullous, pustular, eczematoid; alopecia, nail dystrophy, paronychia
  2. Diarrhoea: Chronic, watery; malabsorption
  3. Alopecia: Total/partial
  4. Mood changes: Apathy, irritability, depression

Other: Hypogonadism, growth retardation, immune dysfunction (candidiasis, recurrent infections), ophthalmic (blepharitis, conjunctivitis, photophobia), taste/smell loss.

Biochemistry: ↓Plasma Zn <70 µg/dL; ↓Alkaline phosphatase (Zn-dependent); low sweat Zn.

Treatment: Zn supplementation 3 mg/kg/day elemental (lifelong); clinical response in 5–10 days; monitor plasma Zn 3-monthly.

5. Section 5: Acquired Zinc Deficiency

CategoryExamples
MalabsorptionCoeliac, Crohn's, short bowel, bariatric (RYGB), CF, biliary atresia
Chronic diseaseLiver cirrhosis (↑renal Zn loss), CKD/dialysis, DM (hyperzincuria)
DrugsPenicillamine (chelation), diuretics, ACE inhibitors, antimetabolites, OCP
↑ DemandPregnancy, lactation, premature infants, growth, burns, surgery, sepsis
DietVegan, alcoholism, elderly ("tea-and-toast"), anorexia, formula without Zn
GeneticAE (ZIP4), acrodermatitis enteropathica, MTF1 mutations (rare)
HepaticAcrodermatitis dysseborrhoica in liver disease (empirical Zn)

Clinical features (acquired):

  • Skin: Perioral/perianal dermatitis, eczema, hyperkeratosis, seborrhoeic rash; similar to AE
  • Hair: Alopecia, brittle hair
  • Nails: Beau's lines, paronychia, onychodystrophy
  • GI: Diarrhoea, anorexia, taste abnormalities
  • Neuro: Depression, irritability, cognitive decline, taste (dysgeusia) and smell (anosmia) loss
  • Immune: ↓T-cell function, lymphopenia, recurrent infections (skin, respiratory, GI)
  • Reproductive: Hypogonadism, ↓spermatogenesis, ↓testosterone, infertility
  • Wound healing: Delayed; collagen synthesis impairment
  • Eye: Night blindness (Zn in retinol dehydrogenase), photophobia

6. Section 6: Zinc in Wilson's Disease

Mechanism: Zn induces metallothionein (MT) in enterocyte; MT has high affinity for Cu; Cu bound to MT is shed into faeces with enterocyte desquamation; ↓Cu absorption (1 mEq/day reduction); mild chelation effect on body Cu.

Indications: Maintenance (chelators first line for acute neurological WD), pregnancy, presymptomatic, children; CHELATE trial (2024): Zn non-inferior to penicillamine for neurological WD.

Dose: Adults 50 mg PO ×3/day; children (5–15y) 25 mg ×3/day; empty stomach (avoid food/iron/calcium reducing absorption).

Time to effect: 6–8 weeks; titrate to 24h urinary Cu <100 µg/day, non-ceruloplasmin Cu <25 µg/dL.

Side effects: GI (nausea, abdominal pain); monitor LFT, Zn levels; rare Cu deficiency with chronic use.

Vs Penicillamine/Trientine: Zn only weakly chelates body Cu; not for active neurological disease; better for maintenance/pregnancy/paediatrics.

7. Section 7: Zinc & Other Therapeutic Uses

UseDoseNotes
Wilson disease50 mg ×3 adult; 25 mg ×3 childInduces MT; empty stomach
Acrodermatitis enteropathica3 mg/kg/day elementalLifelong; titrate to plasma Zn 70–120 µg/dL
Acute diarrhoea (children)10–20 mg/day ×10–14 daysWHO/UNICEF ORS + Zn; ↓duration 25%
Common cold (zinc lozenges)≥75 mg/day (acetate/gluconate)↓duration if started <24h; nasal symptoms; ionic form
Age-related macular degeneration (AMD)25–80 mg/day (AREDS2)Slows progression; Zn + Cu + antioxidants
Sickle cell disease25 mg/day↓pain crises, infections
Recurrent infections in elderly15–30 mg/day↑Immune function (T-cell)
TPN3 mg/day (acute); 12 mg/day (stable)Add to TPN; deficiency 1–2 months
Pica (in pregnancy)30–60 mg/dayReduces pica in Zn-deficient

8. Section 8: Zinc Excess (Toxicity)

Acute: Nausea, vomiting, GI distress, headache, lethargy (single dose 1–2 g); Zn from contaminated food/water (galvanised containers, high-zinc denture cream).

Chronic (industrial, ZnO fumes): Metal fume fever (acute, self-limiting, 12–48h), copper deficiency, ↓immune function, ↓HDL, sideroblastic anaemia (Cu depletes).

Drug interactions: Penicillamine, EDTA (chelate Zn); quinolones/tetracyclines ↓absorption (separate by 2h); PPIs ↓Zn absorption (long-term).

High-dose supplement risks: >100 mg/day → GI upset, Cu deficiency, immune impairment.

9. Section 9: Special Populations & Clinical Scenarios

Pregnancy/lactation: ↑Demand; Zn deficiency → pre-eclampsia, IUGR, preterm birth, neurodevelopmental delay; supplementation 15–30 mg/day; IZiNCG recommends 15 mg in pregnancy.

Elderly: Poor diet, polypharmacy (PPIs, diuretics), chronic disease → marginal status; supplementation 15–30 mg/day may improve immune function.

HIV: Zn deficiency common; supplementation may slow disease progression but excess → viral replication (theoretical).

Malaria: Zn supplementation reduces severity in children (P. falciparum); WHO recommends in endemic areas.

Pneumonia: Zn deficiency → ↑susceptibility, severity; supplementation reduces incidence in children.

4. 4. Clinical Correlation

ScenarioActionNotes
6m infant, perioral/perianal dermatitis, alopecia, diarrhoea, irritabilitySLC39A4 testing (AE); Zn 3 mg/kg/day; monitor plasma Zn; lifelongClassic AE
50F, RYGB 2y ago, alopecia, taste loss, poor wound healing, recurrent infectionsPlasma Zn + alkaline phosphatase; Zn 50–100 mg/day; bariatric multivitaminAcquired Zn deficiency
30M, Wilson's disease, stable, on chelator, planning pregnancySwitch to Zn 50 mg ×3/day; counsel on Zn use in pregnancy; LFT, 24h urinary CuZn safe in pregnancy; chelators teratogenic
65M, alcohol, dermatitis, hypogonadism, ↓tasteZn 50–100 mg/day; alcohol cessation; thiamine, folate, B12Alcoholic Zn deficiency
5y child, severe acute diarrhoea in LMICORS + Zn 10–20 mg/day ×10–14 days; per WHO/UNICEF↓diarrhoea duration 25%, ↓subsequent episodes
70F, AMD, smoking history, vision lossAREDS2 formula: Zn 25 mg + Cu 2 mg + antioxidants (vit C, E, lutein, zeaxanthin)Slows progression to advanced AMD
20F, vegan, anorexia, restrictive diet, hypogeusia, alopeciaZn 50 mg/day; multivitamin; nutritional rehabMultiple deficiencies (Zn, Fe, B12)
40M, SCD, recurrent vaso-occlusive crisesZn 25 mg/day; hydroxyurea; folic acid; analgesiaZn ↓pain crises, infections

5. 5. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- Must know: 2nd most abundant trace element; structural (zinc fingers), catalytic (300+ metalloenzymes), regulatory; RDA 8 F/11 M mg; AE = SLC39A4 (ZIP4) → dermatitis + alopecia + diarrhoea; treatment Zn 3 mg/kg/day; acquired deficiency (malabsorption, alcohol, RYGB, TPN); Wilson's disease (Zn 50 mg ×3); immunity, wound healing, hypogonadism
- Common viva: Acrodermatitis enteropathica (triad + ZIP4 mutation); Zn deficiency dermatitis (perioral/perianal); Wilson's disease Zn mechanism; zinc in diarrhoea (WHO ORS+Zn); zinc fingers in transcription factors; hypogonadism in Zn deficiency
- Exam trap: Confusing Zn with biotin deficiency (both have dermatitis); not recognising AE triad; missing Zn in bariatric workup; giving Zn with food (↓absorption); not using Zn in Wilson's; not recognising hypogonadism in Zn deficiency

6. 6. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
Acrodermatitis enteropathica = iron deficiencyAE = ZIP4 Zn transporter mutation; presents after weaning (breast milk Zn compensates)
Zn deficiency = pellagraNiacin: photosensitive Casal's necklace; Zn: periorificial, not sun-exposed
Wilson's disease: Zn chelates body CuZn induces MT → blocks Cu absorption (enterocyte); mild chelation only; not acute WD
All dermatitis in alcohol = pellagraZn deficiency also presents in alcohol (↓intake, ↑urinary loss, ↓absorption)
Oral Zn supplement with foodEmpty stomach (avoid food/Ca/Fe/quinolones) — chelation/competition
Zinc supplement for everyone with low appetiteTaste loss (dysgeusia) = classic Zn deficiency; supplement if confirmed
Acute diarrhoea Rx = ORS aloneWHO: ORS + Zn 10–20 mg/day ×10–14 days (all children)
AMD prevention: vitamins onlyAREDS2: Zn 25 + Cu 2 + vit C/E + lutein/zeaxanthin; Zn essential
Zn supplementation is always safeExcess → Cu deficiency, ↓immune, sideroblastic anaemia; UL 40 mg/day

7. 7. Mnemonics

  • Zn functions: SCR = Structural (Zn fingers), Catalytic (metalloenzymes), Regulatory (transcription, immune)
  • AE triad: DDA = Dermatitis (periorificial), Diarrhoea, Alopecia
  • AE genetics: ZIP4 = AE; ZIP4 (SLC39A4) on enterocyte
  • Wilson's Rx: Zinc = Zeal for Wilson; 50 mg ×3, empty stomach, induces MT
  • ORS + Zn: WHO in acute diarrhoea in children
  • Zinc excess: GICS = GI upset, Immune ↓, Cu deficiency, Sideroblastic anaemia
  • Zinc deficiency clinical: DAWHIT = Dermatitis, Alopecia, Wound healing ↓, Hypogonadism, Immune ↓, Taste/smell loss
  • Sources high to low: OBEE = Oysters, Beef, Eggs, Everything else (animal > plant)
  • RDA: 8/11 = 8 mg F / 11 mg M; UL 40 mg/day
  • Inhibitors: PHO-FC = Phytate, High Ca, Oxalate, Fe, Cu (compete)

8. 8. Mind Map

Zinc
Body: 2-3g (60% muscle, 30% bone)
Absorption: ZIP4 (SLC39A4) enterocyte
Plasma: Albumin 60%, α2-MG 30%
Excretion: Pancreatic 30%, Intestinal
Functions
Structural: Zn fingers (300+ proteins)
Catalytic: 300+ metalloenzymes
Regulatory: Transcription, immune, signal
RDA: 8 F/11 M mg; UL 40 mg
Acrodermatitis Enteropathica
ZIP4 mutation (SLC39A4)
Triad: Dermatitis + Diarrhoea + Alopecia
Periorificial dermatitis
Rx: Zn 3 mg/kg/day lifelong
Acquired Deficiency
Malabsorption: Coeliac, CF, RYGB
Chronic: Liver, dialysis, alcohol
Drugs: Penicillamine, diuretics
Diet: Vegan, elderly
Demand: Pregnancy, burns
Clinical Features
Dermatitis (perioral/perianal)
Alopecia, onychodystrophy
Diarrhoea
Hypogonadism, ↓testosterone
Taste/smell loss
Immune ↓, T-cell dysfunction
Wound healing ↓
Night blindness (retinol dehydrogenase)
Wilson's Disease
Zn induces MT in enterocyte
Cu bound to MT shed in faeces
Dose: 50 mg ×3 adult; 25 mg ×3 child
Empty stomach; monitor
Maintenance/pregnancy
Other Uses
Acute diarrhoea: ORS + Zn
Common cold: ≥75 mg/day
AMD: AREDS2
SCD: ↓pain crises
Toxicity
Acute: Nausea, vomiting
Chronic: Cu deficiency
Sideroblastic anaemia
↓Immune

9. 9. -Hour Recall Prompts

  1. Body 2-3g: 60% muscle, 30% bone; ZIP4 (SLC39A4) absorption
  2. Functions: Structural (Zn fingers), Catalytic (300+ metalloenzymes), Regulatory (transcription, immune)
  3. AE: ZIP4 mutation, triad (dermatitis + diarrhoea + alopecia), Rx 3 mg/kg/day
  4. Acquired deficiency: malabsorption, alcohol, RYGB, TPN
  5. Wilson's: Zn induces MT → ↓Cu absorption; 50 mg ×3 adult, 25 mg ×3 child, empty stomach
  6. Other uses: ORS+Zn in diarrhoea, AMD (AREDS2), SCD
  7. Toxicity: Cu deficiency, ↓immune, sideroblastic anaemia, UL 40 mg/day
  8. Clinical: dermatitis, alopecia, taste loss, hypogonadism, immune ↓, wound healing
